proposals.title: Re-evaluating Dietary Selenium Requirements for Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus): A Comparative Study of Purified and Practical Ingredient-Based Diets
proposals.motivation: Nutrient requirement studies for fish, including selenium, have historically relied on purified diets to establish baseline values. However, pre-2026 evidence suggests that nutrient bioavailability and metabolism can be significantly altered by the complex matrix of practical, ingredient-based diets used in aquaculture. This discrepancy may lead to suboptimal or inaccurate dietary formulations, impacting growth, antioxidant activity, and immune response in commercially important species like Nile tilapia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dietary selenium requirement for optimal growth performance, tissue mineralization, and antioxidant status in juvenile Nile tilapia will be significantly lower in a practical, ingredient-based diet compared to a purified diet due to differences in selenium bioavailability and interactive effects from other dietary components.
proposals.method: A dose-response study will be conducted using juvenile Nile tilapia. Two basal diets will be formulated: a casein-gelatin-based purified diet and a practical diet based on fishmeal, cornstarch, and soybean meal. Each basal diet will be supplemented with graded levels of selenium (as sodium selenite) to create seven isonitrogenous and isoenergetic experimental diets per series. Growth performance (weight gain, FCR), tissue selenium concentration (liver, muscle), antioxidant enzyme activity (glutathione peroxidase, superoxide dismutase), and immune parameters (lysozyme activity, respiratory burst) will be analyzed after an 8-week feeding trial. Broken-line regression analysis will be used to determine the requirement levels for each diet type.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical, direct comparison of selenium requirements across diet paradigms, offering more applicable data for commercial feed formulation. It will advance the field of fish nutrition by challenging the assumption that purified diet-derived requirements are directly transferable, thereby improving diet efficiency and fish health in practical aquaculture.
proposals.novelty: While selenium requirements have been studied, a direct, controlled comparison of its bioavailability and requirement between purified and practical diets for tilapia is lacking. This study uniquely integrates the researcher's expertise in dietary supplements, antioxidant activity, and nanomaterial synthesis (for potential future delivery systems) to address a fundamental methodological question in nutrient requirement research.

proposals.title: Life-Stage Specific Selenium Requirements in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus): A Dose-Response Study on Growth, Antioxidant Status, and Immune Function
proposals.motivation: Selenium (Se) is a critical micronutrient for antioxidant defense and immune function in fish, yet its dietary requirement for Nile tilapia, a globally significant aquaculture species, is often generalized across life stages. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Wang's work on zinc and polyphenol nanoparticles, highlights the importance of precise mineral nutrition and nano-delivery for fish health. Determining stage-specific Se requirements is essential for formulating cost-effective, health-optimized diets that support larval survival, grow-out performance, and broodstock reproductive quality.
proposals.hypothesis: The optimal dietary selenium requirement for Nile tilapia differs significantly between larval, grow-out, and broodstock life stages, with higher requirements anticipated during periods of rapid growth (larvae) and reproductive investment (broodstock) due to increased oxidative stress and metabolic demand.
proposals.method: We will conduct three parallel dose-response feeding trials using semi-purified diets supplemented with graded levels (0, 0.15, 0.3, 0.6, 1.2 mg Se/kg) of selenomethionine. Larval, juvenile (grow-out), and mature broodstock tilapia will be reared in separate, recirculating systems. Key response variables will include growth performance (weight gain, SGR), tissue Se concentration (via ICP-MS), antioxidant activity (SOD, GPx, CAT assays), and immune parameters (lysozyme, respiratory burst). Data will be analyzed using broken-line or quadratic regression to determine the optimal requirement for each life stage.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, stage-specific selenium requirement data for Nile tilapia, directly informing precision feed formulation. It will elucidate the link between Se intake, antioxidant capacity, and immune resilience across ontogeny, contributing to improved aquaculture sustainability and animal welfare.
proposals.novelty: While Se requirements exist for generic 'tilapia,' this systematically compares three distinct physiological stages within one species, integrating modern mineral analysis with functional health biomarkers, building upon Dr. Wang's expertise in mineral supplementation and nano-bioactive delivery for aquaculture.

proposals.title: Comparative Efficacy of Selenite and Selenomethionine on Growth, Selenium Retention, and Antioxidant-Immune Modulation in Hybrid Grouper (Epinephelus fuscoguttatus ♀ × E. lanceolatus ♂)
proposals.motivation: In aquaculture, selenium (Se) is a critical micronutrient for antioxidant defense and immune function, with Se-methionine (Se-Met) widely studied as an efficient organic source. However, its high cost limits practical application. Pre-2026 research lacks a direct, systematic comparison of cost-effective inorganic selenite with Se-Met in high-value species like hybrid grouper, particularly regarding optimal inclusion levels and impacts on tissue-specific retention and metabolic pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dietary selenite, at an optimized inclusion level, can achieve comparable growth performance, tissue selenium deposition, and enhancement of antioxidant and immune status in hybrid grouper to Se-Met, by effectively modulating key selenoprotein expression and energy metabolism pathways.
proposals.method: A 10-week feeding trial will be conducted using a 2 × 3 factorial design: two Se sources (sodium selenite and Se-Met) at three graded levels (0.2, 0.4, and 0.6 mg Se/kg diet), plus a Se-deficient control. Growth performance (weight gain, FCR) and tissue (muscle, liver, kidney) Se concentration will be measured. Antioxidant activity (SOD, GPx, CAT) and immune parameters (lysozyme, complement activity) in serum and liver will be assessed. Hepatic transcript levels of key selenoproteins (e.g., GPx1, SelP) and genes related to energy metabolism (inspired by recent work on bacterial energy inhibition) will be quantified via qPCR. Data will be analyzed using two-way ANOVA.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a clear, evidence-based comparison of selenium source efficacy, establishing optimal and economical dietary Se levels for hybrid grouper aquaculture. It will elucidate the mechanistic links between Se source, selenoprotein expression, and the antioxidant-immune axis, contributing to refined nutritional strategies for sustainable fish farming.
proposals.novelty: While Se sources have been compared in tilapia, a direct, multi-level comparison in the metabolically distinct hybrid grouper, integrating tissue-specific retention with molecular analysis of selenoprotein and energy metabolism genes, represents a novel and targeted approach to selenium nutrition.

proposals.title: Chronic Selenosis in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus): A Comprehensive Study of Tissue Accumulation, Pathological Alterations, and Antioxidant System Dysregulation Following Long-Term Dietary Selenium Excess
proposals.motivation: Selenium (Se) is an essential micronutrient in aquafeeds, but the narrow margin between requirement and toxicity poses a significant risk in intensive aquaculture. While acute selenium toxicity is documented, pre-2026 knowledge lacks detailed, long-term studies on the chronic pathological effects (selenosis) in commercially vital fish like Nile tilapia, particularly under practical farming conditions. Understanding these chronic impacts is crucial for formulating safe, sustainable diets and preventing subclinical production losses and welfare issues.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that long-term feeding of supra-optimal dietary selenium levels will lead to dose-dependent accumulation in key tissues (liver, kidney, muscle), induce specific histopathological lesions, and cause a biphasic disruption of the antioxidant system, ultimately compromising growth and health in Nile tilapia.
proposals.method: A 16-week feeding trial will be conducted with juvenile Nile tilapia. Five isonitrogenous and isoenergetic diets will be formulated: a control with adequate Se (1 mg/kg, as selenomethionine) and four treatments with graded supra-optimal levels (5, 10, 15, 20 mg/kg). Growth performance will be monitored. Post-trial, fish will be sampled for ICP-MS analysis of Se accumulation in liver, kidney, and muscle. Histopathological examination (H&E staining) of liver, kidney, and gill tissues will be performed. Antioxidant activity (SOD, CAT, GPx) and lipid peroxidation (MDA levels) will be assayed in liver homogenates. Immune parameters (lysozyme, respiratory burst) will also be evaluated.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a detailed dose-response model for chronic selenium toxicity in a major aquaculture species, defining tissue-specific accumulation patterns and histological biomarkers of selenosis. It will elucidate the long-term dynamics of antioxidant defense under selenium excess, contributing critical data for refining maximum tolerable level (MTL) guidelines in fish nutrition and informing risk assessments for feed formulations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating Wang's expertise in mineral nutrition, nanoparticle synthesis, and metabolic analysis to investigate chronic, sub-lethal selenium toxicity—a less-studied area compared to deficiency or acute poisoning—using a holistic approach that links tissue-specific pathology with systemic oxidative and immune responses in tilapia.

proposals.title: Dietary Selenium Sparing by Vitamin E and Nano-Encapsulated Dihydromyricetin: Assessing Antioxidant Interactions and Growth Performance in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus)
proposals.motivation: In aquaculture, optimizing micronutrient supplementation is crucial for cost-effective, sustainable feed formulation. Selenium (Se) and vitamin E (VE) are known to interact synergistically in antioxidant defense, but the quantitative dietary Se requirement in fish under varying VE and novel antioxidant levels remains poorly defined. This study addresses this gap using a relevant aquaculture species, Nile tilapia, to refine nutritional models and reduce potential mineral waste.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that elevated dietary levels of vitamin E and nano-encapsulated dihydromyricetin (DMY) will spare the dietary selenium requirement in Nile tilapia, as evidenced by maintained growth performance, tissue mineralization, and antioxidant status at lower Se inclusion levels.
proposals.method: A 3x3 factorial design will be employed, with three dietary Se levels (deficient, adequate, supra-nutritional as selenomethionine) and three antioxidant regimes (basal VE, high VE, high VE + DMY-loaded nanoliposomes coated with tea saponin). Juvenile Nile tilapia will be fed the experimental diets for 8 weeks. Growth performance (weight gain, FCR), tissue Se and zinc mineralization, and systemic antioxidant activity (SOD, GPx, MDA levels) will be measured. Immune response (lysozyme, respiratory burst) and hepatic gene expression of selenoproteins and antioxidant enzymes will be analyzed to elucidate the molecular interaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative model of the Se-VE-DMY interaction in a major aquaculture species, directly informing precise feed formulation. It will contribute novel data on how advanced antioxidant delivery systems (nanoliposomes) influence traditional micronutrient requirements, bridging nutritional biochemistry and nanomaterial applications in aquaculture.
proposals.novelty: While Se-VE interactions are known, this study uniquely integrates a third, nano-delivered phenolic antioxidant (DMY) into the interaction model. It leverages the researcher's established expertise in zinc nanoparticles, nanoliposomes, and tilapia nutrition to investigate a classic nutritional question with contemporary nano-biotechnological tools.

proposals.title: Pharmacokinetic Modeling of Selenium Dynamics in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus): A Compartmental Approach to Optimize Dietary Supplementation
proposals.motivation: Selenium is an essential trace element for fish, crucial for antioxidant defense and immune function, but its narrow therapeutic window makes precise dosing critical. Current aquaculture practices rely on static dietary inclusion rates, which fail to account for dynamic absorption, tissue-specific distribution, and elimination kinetics, potentially leading to deficiency or toxicity. Applying formal pharmacokinetic (PK) models, a standard in pharmacology, to fish nutrition represents a significant, untapped opportunity to predict and optimize selenium status for health and growth.
proposals.hypothesis: The absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (ADME) of dietary selenium in Nile tilapia can be accurately described by a multi-compartment pharmacokinetic model, and this model will predict that nanoparticle-chelated selenium formulations, inspired by recent zinc delivery systems, significantly enhance bioavailability and tissue retention compared to conventional supplements.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled feeding trial with Nile tilapia, administering a single oral dose of selenium (as selenite and as a novel, bio-conditioned selenium nanoparticle synthesized analogously to prior zinc work). Serial blood and tissue (liver, muscle, kidney) samples will be collected over 96 hours. Data will be fitted using nonlinear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM) to establish a base two-compartment model (central plasma, peripheral tissue). Model selection (e.g., adding enterohepatic recirculation) will be guided by Akaike Information Criterion. The final validated model will simulate long-term retention and optimal dosing regimens.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, predictive PK model for selenium in a key aquaculture species, moving beyond empirical dose-response studies. It will establish a methodological framework for applying pharmacological modeling to aquatic trace element nutrition. The results will directly inform the rational design of advanced, nano-enabled selenium supplements to improve bioavailability and reduce environmental waste.
proposals.novelty: While PK modeling is mature in human/terrestrial animal pharmacology, its application to predict mineral dynamics in fish is novel. Integrating this approach with the design of next-generation nanoparticle supplements (extending Wang's expertise with zinc and dihydromyricetin nanoformulations) to explicitly test and model bioavailability kinetics is a significant innovation in aquaculture nutrition science.

proposals.title: Integration of a Nano-Zinc Optimum into a Least-Cost Linear Programming Model for Commercial Tilapia Feed: Effects on Growth, Health, and Economic Viability
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Wang et al. identified a precise dietary zinc supplement (0.57 mg/kg from gluconic acid zinc or nano-formulations) that optimizes growth and immune function in Nile tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) under experimental conditions. Translating this precise nutrient optimum into a practical, cost-effective commercial feed is a critical bottleneck for sustainable aquaculture. This project addresses the gap between laboratory efficacy and scalable, economically viable feed manufacturing.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating the identified 0.57 mg/kg bioavailable zinc optimum into a least-cost feed formulation using linear programming will maintain or enhance the growth performance and health benefits observed in small-scale studies, while demonstrating economic feasibility for large-scale tilapia farming.
proposals.method: We will first characterize the nutrient composition and market price of candidate feed ingredients (e.g., cornstarch, fishmeal, soybean meal, vitamin/mineral premixes). Using a linear programming algorithm (e.g., the simplex method implemented in Python's SciPy or dedicated feed formulation software), we will construct a least-cost model with nutritional constraints (protein, lipid, amino acids) and a new constraint for the 0.57 mg/kg bioavailable zinc from the specified supplement. The formulated feed will be tested in a large-scale pond trial with Nile tilapia, comparing growth performance (weight gain, FCR), tissue mineralization, and immune/antioxidant activity markers against a standard commercial diet.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, commercially scalable feed formulation that integrates a precise nano-mineral optimum, directly bridging nutraceutical research and aquaculture practice. It will deliver a concrete framework for incorporating novel, performance-enhancing supplements into economic optimization models for feed mills.
proposals.novelty: While least-cost formulation is standard, the integration of a precisely defined, low-dose nano/bio-chelated mineral optimum (derived from advanced nutraceutical studies) as a fixed constraint in the economic model is novel. This moves beyond testing additives in isolation to solving the practical problem of their cost-effective inclusion in complex ingredient matrices.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Selenium Threshold: A Dose-Response Model for Growth, Oxidative Stress, and Immune Modulation in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus)
proposals.motivation: Selenium (Se) is an essential micronutrient for fish, crucial for antioxidant selenoprotein function, but exhibits a narrow margin between dietary requirement and toxicity. In aquaculture, precise Se supplementation is critical for optimizing growth and health, yet the exact threshold where it switches from promoter to inhibitor remains poorly quantified, especially in the context of modern nano-delivery systems. This project addresses the need for a predictive model to guide safe and effective Se use in aquafeeds.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the growth response of Nile tilapia to dietary selenium follows a hormetic dose-response curve, where low doses enhance growth and immune function via improved antioxidant activity, but a specific threshold exists beyond which oxidative stress and metabolic disruption lead to growth inhibition and tissue damage.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 10-week feeding trial with juvenile Nile tilapia, using a cornstarch-based semi-purified diet supplemented with sodium selenite and selenium nanoparticles at six graded concentrations (0 to 5 mg Se/kg diet). Growth performance (weight gain, FCR), tissue mineralization (Se accumulation in liver and muscle), and health biomarkers (plasma glutathione peroxidase activity, liver malondialdehyde levels, lysozyme activity) will be measured. A broken-line regression model and a quadratic polynomial model will be statistically fitted to the growth and biomarker data to identify the inflection point (breakpoint) and optimal dose.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a precise, quantitative model of the selenium toxicity threshold for a key aquaculture species, directly informing feed formulation. It will elucidate the underlying physiological shift from antioxidant support to pro-oxidant damage. The comparison of selenite and nano-Se forms will contribute to the emerging field of nanonutrition in aquaculture.
proposals.novelty: While Se requirements are known, this proposal uniquely integrates growth, mineral deposition, and a suite of oxidative stress and immune biomarkers to model the exact toxicological breakpoint. It applies advanced dose-response modeling, common in toxicology, to a practical aquaculture nutrition problem, bridging the gap between nutrient requirement and contaminant risk assessment.

proposals.title: Inverse Modeling of Dietary Selenium Intake in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) Using Tissue Biomarkers and a Hybrid Pharmacokinetic-Machine Learning Algorithm
proposals.motivation: Accurate assessment of past dietary selenium (Se) intake is critical for optimizing aquaculture nutrition and health, as both deficiency and excess impair fish growth, antioxidant capacity, and immune response. Current methods rely on direct feed analysis or controlled feeding trials, which are impractical for retrospective evaluation in farm settings. An inverse model to predict intake from tissue concentrations would revolutionize nutritional diagnostics and feed formulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid model integrating a one-compartment pharmacokinetic (PK) framework with a machine learning regressor can accurately estimate historical dietary selenium intake in Nile tilapia from a single measurement of liver or muscle selenium concentration.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled feeding trial with Nile tilapia fed graded levels of selenium (as selenomethionine) over 12 weeks, periodically sampling serum, liver, and muscle for Se analysis via ICP-MS. A foundational PK model (dC/dt = k_in * I - k_out * C) will describe Se dynamics. We will then train a Gradient Boosting Regression (GBR) algorithm (e.g., XGBoost, pre-2026) using features derived from the PK model (e.g., estimated clearance rate, time-weighted exposure) and biological covariates (fish weight, growth rate) to predict the dietary intake level. Model performance will be validated on a held-out cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated algorithm for retrospective Se intake estimation in aquaculture, directly applicable to nutritional status assessment and precision feeding. It will establish a novel methodological framework that combines mechanistic PK modeling with data-driven ML, extendable to other trace minerals, thereby advancing the field of nutritional bioinformatics in animal science.
proposals.novelty: While forward models (diet to tissue) exist, the inverse problem is rarely tackled in fish nutrition. The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of a physiological PK model with a modern ML regressor to solve this inverse estimation, moving beyond simple correlation to a causal inference-based prediction tool.

proposals.title: Quantifying Selenium Flux in Tilapia Aquaculture: A Mass-Balance Model from Feed to Pond Sediment
proposals.motivation: Selenium (Se) is an essential micronutrient in aquafeeds, but its environmental fate in aquaculture ponds is poorly understood. Excessive Se from uneaten feed and waste can accumulate in sediments, posing a risk of remobilization and toxicity to aquatic ecosystems. This study addresses a critical gap in sustainable aquaculture by tracing Se flow, linking Wang's expertise in fish nutrition and nanoparticle synthesis to environmental chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant fraction of dietary selenium supplemented in organic forms (e.g., selenomethionine) in tilapia feed will be retained in fish biomass, while the remainder will partition into pond water and sediments, with its speciation and bioavailability altered by microbial activity and redox conditions.
proposals.method: We will conduct an 8-week feeding trial with Nile tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) using feeds supplemented with isotopically labeled 77Se (as selenomethionine). A mass-balance model will be constructed using STELLA software to quantify Se fluxes. Key parameters—including Se concentration in feed, fish tissues (muscle, liver), water (dissolved and particulate), and sediments—will be measured via ICP-MS. Sediment microcosm experiments will assess Se speciation (using HPLC-ICP-MS) and microbial inhibition potential, linking to Wang's prior work on antimicrobial nanoparticles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative model of Se cycling in a tilapia pond system, directly informing feed formulation to minimize environmental impact. It will establish a foundational framework for assessing the ecological risk of trace element supplements in aquaculture and identify potential hotspots for Se sequestration or release.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating isotopic tracing with a dynamic ecosystem model specifically for Se in aquaculture, and in connecting dietary supplement bioavailability directly to sediment biogeochemistry—a link not established in prior tilapia nutrition studies focused solely on growth performance.

proposals.title: Directed In Vitro Evolution of Probiotic Bacillus spp. for Enhanced Persistence in the Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) Gut via a Dynamic Gastrointestinal Tract Simulator
proposals.motivation: The efficacy of dietary probiotics in aquaculture is often limited by poor survival in the host's gastrointestinal tract, leading to inconsistent growth and health outcomes. Current in vitro selection methods fail to replicate the complex, dynamic stressors of the fish gut environment. This gap necessitates a more predictive, engineering-based approach to probiotic development to improve feed efficiency and disease resistance in species like Nile tilapia.
proposals.hypothesis: That serial passage of probiotic Bacillus candidates in a sophisticated, dynamic in vitro simulator replicating key tilapia GI stressors (e.g., bile salts, lysozyme, low pH, microbial competition) will select for pre-adapted strains with genetically stable, enhanced persistence and colonization capacity in vivo, directly improving host performance metrics.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-chamber, continuous-culture gastrointestinal tract simulator (GITS) for Nile tilapia, programmed with sequential pH profiles, pulses of bile salts and lysozyme, and a background of resident gut microflora. Candidate Bacillus strains (e.g., B. subtilis) will be subjected to 30+ serial passages in this system. Survivors will be isolated, and their genomes sequenced to identify adaptive mutations. The top pre-adapted strain and a non-adapted control will be microencapsulated in a cornstarch-zein matrix and tested in a feeding trial with O. niloticus, assessing growth performance, gut colonization (via qPCR), and immune-antioxidant status.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, high-throughput platform for the rational design of host-specific probiotics, moving beyond random screening. It will provide a genetically characterized, high-persistence probiotic strain for tilapia aquaculture and establish a generalizable methodology linking directed in vitro evolution to guaranteed in vivo performance.
proposals.novelty: While serial passage is common in microbial evolution, its application to pre-adapt probiotics using a dynamically simulated, host-specific gut environment is novel. This integrates environmental engineering principles with aquaculture nutrition, creating a predictive selection guarantee rather than a passive survival assay.

proposals.title: Dietary Modulation of Probiotic *Lactobacillus* Efficacy in Nile Tilapia (*Oreochromis niloticus*): A Mechanistic Multi-Omics Study on the Role of Zinc Sources and Prebiotic Fibers
proposals.motivation: The efficacy of dietary probiotics in aquaculture is highly variable, often attributed to poorly defined host-diet-microbiome interactions. Pre-2026, while studies show probiotics can improve fish growth and immunity, the specific dietary components that mechanistically enable or inhibit their function remain unknown, hindering the development of reliable, synergistic feed formulations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the functional efficacy of a defined probiotic (*Lactobacillus* sp.) in Nile tilapia is directly modulated by specific dietary zinc sources (e.g., gluconic acid zinc, zinc nanoparticles) and prebiotic fibers (e.g., cornstarch derivatives), which alter the gut environment to either support or suppress probiotic activity and host response.
proposals.method: We will use a gnotobiotic Nile tilapia model fed modular, isocaloric diets in a 2x3 factorial design, varying zinc source (ZnO, gluconic acid zinc, poly-dihydromyricetin zinc nanoparticles) and fiber (cornstarch, modified cornstarch). Fish will be colonized with a defined probiotic strain. Integrated multi-omics (16S rRNA gene sequencing, metatranscriptomics, and LC-MS metabolomics) of gut contents will be coupled with host endpoints: growth performance, tissue mineralization, serum immune markers (lysozyme, complement activity), and antioxidant status. Data will be integrated using multivariate statistical modeling (e.g., sparse Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis) and network inference to map causal dietary-probiotic-host interaction pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond correlative observations to establish causal dietary prerequisites for probiotic function. It will yield a predictive model linking specific feed components to probiotic metabolic activity and host benefits, directly informing the rational design of next-generation aquafeeds. The study will also advance the application of nano-mineral supplements and prebiotics in sustainable aquaculture.
proposals.novelty: This is the first study to apply a gnotobiotic fish model and factorial multi-omics design to deconvolve the specific, mechanistic roles of advanced zinc supplements and prebiotic fibers in modulating probiotic efficacy, bridging Wang's expertise in nano-delivery, mineral nutrition, and antibacterial compounds with probiotic-host interaction science.

proposals.title: Decoupling Diet-Dependent Effects on Probiotic Efficacy in Nile Tilapia Using a Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic Modeling Framework
proposals.motivation: In aquaculture, probiotic efficacy is highly variable, often attributed to interactions with the host's basal diet, but this relationship is poorly quantified. This uncertainty hinders the development of robust, universally effective probiotic supplements. A method to isolate the intrinsic activity of a probiotic from dietary confounders is critically needed to advance precision nutrition in fish farming.
proposals.hypothesis: The core pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic (PK-PD) relationship of a defined probiotic strain, linking its gut persistence (PK) to a measurable health outcome (PD), remains invariant across systematically varied dietary compositions, indicating a diet-independent mechanism of action.
proposals.method: We will use Nile tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) and a characterized probiotic (e.g., Lactobacillus sp.). A central composite design will create diets varying in key components (protein source, lipid level, and a zinc supplement based on gluconic acid zinc, aligning with Wang's expertise). The PK component will be quantified via qPCR of probiotic-specific genes in digesta over time. The PD component will be a key performance indicator like specific growth rate or an immune marker (e.g., lysozyme activity). A non-linear mixed-effects PK-PD model (e.g., an Emax model linked to a PK compartment model) will be fitted to data from each diet. Model parameter invariance across diets will be statistically tested.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework to test if a probiotic's action is fundamentally diet-modulated. It will deliver a novel modeling tool for the aquaculture industry to evaluate probiotic candidates more reliably. The findings will clarify whether nutritional strategies should focus on supporting the probiotic or selecting robust strains.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying a clinical pharmacology PK-PD framework, standard for drug evaluation, to the problem of diet-probiotic interactions in animal nutrition. It moves beyond correlation to model causality, explicitly decoupling the probiotic's intrinsic efficacy from the dietary matrix.

proposals.title: Diet-Independent Efficacy of a Novel Probiotic in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus): A Comparative Assessment Across Basal Feeds of Varying Protein and Energy Sources
proposals.motivation: In aquaculture, probiotic efficacy is often evaluated using a single, optimized diet, creating uncertainty about its robustness under practical farming conditions where feed composition varies. Determining if a probiotic's benefits on growth, immunity, and gut health are contingent on specific dietary components is critical for developing reliable, universally effective feed supplements. This addresses a key gap in pre-2026 probiotic application research, which largely overlooks diet-probiotic interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: The beneficial effects of a selected probiotic strain on growth performance, antioxidant status, and immune response in Nile tilapia are independent of the protein source and energy density of the basal diet. We hypothesize that the probiotic will elicit consistent, positive outcomes regardless of whether the basal feed is fishmeal-based, plant-protein-based, or formulated with different energy levels.
proposals.method: A 60-day feeding trial will be conducted with juvenile Nile tilapia in a 3x2 factorial design: three iso-nitrogenous but distinct basal diets (Diet A: fishmeal-based control; Diet B: high-plant-protein with cornstarch; Diet C: modified energy density with alternative lipid source) will each be supplemented with or without a proprietary probiotic blend (e.g., Lactobacillus sp. and Bacillus sp.). Growth performance (SGR, FCR), tissue mineralization, serum antioxidant enzymes (SOD, CAT), and immune parameters (lysozyme activity, respiratory burst) will be measured. Data will be analyzed using two-way ANOVA to isolate main effects of diet and probiotic and their interaction.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide definitive evidence on whether a probiotic's action is modulated by diet, a fundamental question for probiotic formulation and recommendation. It will yield practical guidelines for aquaculturists on probiotic use with alternative, cost-effective feeds. The work will also contribute methodologically by establishing a robust framework for testing functional feed additives across diverse nutritional backgrounds.
proposals.novelty: While many studies test probiotics, few systematically dissect the diet-probiotic interaction using feeds with fundamentally different compositions. Leveraging Dr. Wang's expertise in dietary supplements (zinc nanoparticles, gluconic acid zinc) and nanocarriers (nanoliposomes), this proposal uniquely applies a rigorous nutritional experimental design to a microbial intervention, moving beyond single-diet efficacy claims.

proposals.title: Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic Modeling of Dihydromyricetin Nanoparticles to Optimize Dietary Supplementation for Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) Growth and Immunity
proposals.motivation: Optimizing dietary zinc and antioxidant supplements like dihydromyricetin (DMY) in aquaculture is currently empirical, leading to inefficiency and potential waste. A rigorous, quantitative framework for dose optimization is lacking. Translating the established clinical pharmacology paradigm of PK/PD modeling to fish nutrition would provide a principled method to define minimal effective doses and optimal feeding schedules, enhancing precision and sustainability.
proposals.hypothesis: The growth-promoting and immunomodulatory effects of PEGylated DMY-loaded nanoliposomes in Nile tilapia are a direct function of tissue exposure levels, and a PK/PD model can identify the minimal effective tissue concentration and the feeding regimen required to achieve it.
proposals.method: Nile tilapia will be fed diets containing PEGylated DMY-nanoliposomes at graded doses. Serial blood and tissue (e.g., liver, muscle) samples will be collected over time to establish pharmacokinetic profiles using HPLC-MS. Concurrently, pharmacodynamic biomarkers (e.g., specific growth rate, lysozyme activity, hepatic antioxidant enzyme levels) will be measured. Data will be fitted using non-linear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM) to construct a linked PK/PD model, employing an Emax model to relate tissue DMY concentration to effect magnitude.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first PK/PD model for a nano-encapsulated phytochemical feed additive in aquaculture, providing a quantitative rationale for dosage. It will establish a novel methodological framework for precision nutrition, moving beyond growth-based dose-response to include immune and antioxidant endpoints linked to tissue pharmacokinetics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct adaptation of clinical drug development methodology (PK/PD modeling) to the problem of nutritional supplement dosing in fish, integrating the researcher's expertise in nano-delivery, zinc supplementation, and antibacterial activity into a unified quantitative model.

proposals.title: Dose- and Time-Dependent Effects of a Novel Zinc-Gluconate and Dihydromyricetin Nanocomposite on Growth, Immune Function, and Disease Resistance in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus)
proposals.motivation: The aquaculture industry urgently requires precise, cost-effective nutritional strategies to enhance fish health and productivity while minimizing feed costs and potential environmental impacts of nutrient runoff. While dietary zinc and polyphenol supplements like dihydromyricetin (DMY) have shown promise for improving fish growth and immunity, their optimal synergistic dosage and required administration duration remain poorly defined, hindering practical application.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel dietary nanocomposite of zinc-gluconate and DMY will exhibit a dose- and time-dependent effect on Nile tilapia, with a specific minimal effective dose and optimal feeding period that maximizes growth performance, antioxidant capacity, and immune response without causing metabolic overload.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a stable nanocomposite using a modified bio-conditioning method, encapsulating zinc-gluconate and DMY in a cornstarch-based matrix. A factorial design will be employed, testing three graded doses (low, medium, high) of the nanocomposite against a zinc-only control and a basal diet, each administered over three distinct feeding periods (4, 8, and 12 weeks). Key metrics include specific growth rate, feed conversion ratio, tissue zinc deposition, serum lysozyme and complement activity, liver antioxidant enzyme profiles (SOD, CAT), and a final challenge with Aeromonas hydrophila to assess disease resistance.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a precise, economically viable feeding protocol for a novel nano-delivered immunonutrient in tilapia aquaculture. It will establish clear dose-response and time-course relationships, directly informing feed formulation to reduce waste and improve farm profitability. The findings will advance the field of nutritional immunology by elucidating how administration dynamics influence the efficacy of mineral-polyphenol synergies.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond simply proving efficacy of a supplement to rigorously defining its practical application parameters. It uniquely integrates dose- and time-response analyses for a nano-encapsulated mineral-polyphenol complex, a critical step towards commercialization that is often overlooked in preliminary aquaculture nutrition studies.

proposals.title: Shotgun Metagenomic Mining of Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) Gut Microbiota for Functional Prediction of Zinc-Mobilizing and Pathogen-Inhibiting Probiotic Candidates
proposals.motivation: Intensive aquaculture faces challenges from disease outbreaks and suboptimal growth, often addressed by dietary supplements like zinc and antimicrobials. However, sustainable alternatives are needed. The gut microbiome is a key modulator of fish health, but pre-2026 studies often rely on 16S rRNA sequencing, which lacks the functional resolution needed to identify probiotic strains with specific, beneficial metabolic traits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that shotgun metagenomic sequencing of Nile tilapia gut microbiota, under different dietary zinc regimens (conventional vs. organic), will reveal bacterial taxa encoding gene clusters for zinc solubilization (e.g., through gluconic acid production) and for the biosynthesis of antimicrobial compounds like dihydromyricetin derivatives, identifying novel, host-native probiotic candidates.
proposals.method: We will perform shotgun metagenomic sequencing on gut content DNA from Nile tilapia fed a control diet, a zinc oxide diet, and a zinc gluconate diet (n=15/group). Reads will be assembled using MEGAHIT and binned into metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs) with MetaBAT2. Functional annotation will use the KEGG and antiSMASH databases via Prokka and HUMAnN3. We will specifically mine for genes involved in zinc metabolism (e.g., znuABC, gluconate kinase) and secondary metabolite biosynthesis. Candidate taxa will be isolated using selective media and validated in vitro for zinc solubilization and pathogen inhibition against Aeromonas hydrophila.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond correlative microbiome surveys to provide a functional, gene-centric blueprint for discovering next-generation probiotics in aquaculture. It will directly link dietary supplement strategies (zinc forms) to shifts in microbial functional potential. The project will yield a shortlist of culturable, genetically characterized candidate strains with predicted health-promoting activities for further in vivo testing.
proposals.novelty: This integrates Wang's expertise in zinc dietary supplements and nano-encapsulated bioactive compounds (e.g., dihydromyricetin) with a functional metagenomics workflow to prospect for probiotics, a shift from his prior biochemical and nutritional studies. The targeted search for microbes encoding both zinc-mobilizing and antimicrobial traits, informed by his previous findings, is a novel, hypothesis-driven approach to probiotic discovery.

proposals.title: Dose-Dependent Spatiotemporal Mapping of Engineered Probiotic Colonization in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) Using Luminescent Biosensors
proposals.motivation: Probiotic use in aquaculture is widespread, but dosing regimens remain largely empirical, based on growth or survival endpoints rather than a mechanistic understanding of colonization dynamics. This leads to inefficiency and variable efficacy. A pre-2026 gap exists in directly visualizing and quantifying probiotic colonization in real-time within live fish to define a minimal effective colonization threshold for host benefit.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a defined, spatiotemporal colonization pattern of an engineered luminescent Bacillus subtilis 7k probiotic in the tilapia gut, achievable above a specific dose and duration threshold, is a prerequisite for significant modulation of host immune gene expression and improved growth performance.
proposals.method: We will engineer a stable luminescent B. subtilis 7k strain via chromosomal integration of a lux operon. Nile tilapia will be fed diets supplemented with this probiotic at three graded doses (e.g., 10^6, 10^8, 10^10 CFU/g) over 4- and 8-week periods. Colonization will be tracked non-invasively in live fish using an in vivo imaging system (IVIS) to generate spatiotemporal maps. These maps will be correlated with qPCR analysis of gut immune genes (e.g., IL-1β, TNF-α, TGF-β) and standard zootechnical parameters (growth, feed conversion).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, causal link between probiotic dose, colonization dynamics, and host response, moving beyond correlation. It will provide a data-driven framework for determining the minimal effective dose based on achieving a measurable colonization benchmark, optimizing cost-efficiency in aquaculture. The engineered reporter strain serves as a novel tool for real-time monitoring of microbial therapeutics.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of real-time, in vivo luminescent imaging of a precisely engineered probiotic with dose-response host phenotyping in fish is novel. This approach shifts the paradigm from outcome-based dosing to colonization-threshold-based dosing, a concept not yet established in pre-2026 aquaculture probiotic research.

proposals.title: Droplet Microfluidics for Single-Cell Probiotic Encapsulation: Engineering Larval Grouper Gut Microbiome Colonization Dynamics
proposals.motivation: High mortality in larval grouper aquaculture is significantly linked to unstable gut microbiota and disease susceptibility. Pre-2026 probiotic applications use bulk delivery, which is inefficient for larvae and fails to reveal the fundamental, single-cell colonization dynamics critical for establishing protective microbial communities during this vulnerable life stage.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that monodisperse, single-probiotic-cell microcapsules, delivered via live feed, will enable precise tracking and significantly enhance the targeted colonization success and spatial distribution of beneficial bacteria in the larval grouper gut compared to conventional bulk probiotic methods.
proposals.method: We will employ a flow-focusing droplet microfluidic device to generate uniform alginate-based microcapsules, each containing a single cell of a candidate probiotic (e.g., *Lactobacillus* or *Bacillus* strain). Capsules will be coated with a cornstarch-based adhesive layer for bioadhesion and co-fed with rotifers to larval *Epinephelus coioides*. Colonization will be tracked using fluorescent protein-tagged probiotics and confocal microscopy, while host responses will be assessed via assays for immune gene expression (e.g., IL-1β, TNF-α) and antioxidant activity in larval tissues.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel platform for single-cell resolution analysis of probiotic colonization in fish larvae. It will generate foundational knowledge on the spatiotemporal dynamics of gut microbiome engineering, directly linking individual probiotic cell delivery to host physiological outcomes like immune modulation and growth performance.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of single-cell microencapsulation via microfluidics with larval fish nutrition is novel, moving beyond bulk probiotic studies to enable the direct observation and manipulation of colonization at the level of individual microbial cells within a complex host system.

proposals.title: Dietary supplementation with zinc-probiotic nanocomposites enhances growth performance, stress resistance, and gut health in juvenile hybrid grouper (Epinephelus fuscoguttatus × E. lanceolatus)
proposals.motivation: Early life stages of high-value grouper species are critically vulnerable to microbial infections and environmental stress, leading to high mortality in hatcheries and significant economic losses. Pre-2026 research on dietary supplements like zinc and probiotics has focused primarily on adult or sub-adult fish, leaving a knowledge gap for larval and juvenile stages where the developing immune and digestive systems are most sensitive to nutritional intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel dietary supplement combining zinc nanoparticles (synthesized via bio-conditioning with polyphenols like dihydromyricetin) with selected probiotic strains will significantly improve survival, growth, and disease resistance in juvenile grouper by enhancing gut barrier function, antioxidant capacity, and microbial homeostasis.
proposals.method: We will synthesize zinc nanoparticles using a bio-conditioning method with dihydromyricetin (as in Wang's prior work) and encapsulate them with a Lactobacillus sp. probiotic in PEGylated nanoliposomes coated with tea saponin for targeted gut delivery. Juvenile hybrid grouper will be fed one of four diets: control, zinc nanocomposite only, probiotic only, and zinc-probiotic nanocomposite. Growth performance (weight gain, SGR), tissue mineralization, antioxidant activity (SOD, CAT, MDA levels), immune response (lysozyme, TNF-α), and gut microbiome analysis (16S rRNA sequencing) will be assessed before and after a Vibrio challenge test.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first efficacy data on a synergistic zinc-probiotic nano-delivery system for marine fish larvae, directly addressing a major bottleneck in grouper aquaculture. It will establish optimal delivery mechanisms for immunonutrients in early life stages and contribute practical protocols for improving hatchery survival rates.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted application of an advanced, multi-functional nanocomposite (merging Zn bioavailability, probiotic viability, and pathogen-inhibiting saponin) specifically to the critical juvenile stage, a scale-down from prior adult-focused studies, and the systematic evaluation of its dual role in enhancing both antioxidant defense and microbial ecology.

proposals.title: A Bioreactor Scale-Up Framework for Predicting Probiotic Efficacy in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) Aquaculture
proposals.motivation: Probiotic applications in aquaculture often fail to translate from controlled lab studies to commercial-scale ponds due to unpredictable environmental interactions. This inconsistency limits their adoption as sustainable alternatives to antibiotics. A systematic, engineering-based scaling methodology is urgently needed to bridge this lab-to-field gap and ensure reliable probiotic performance.
proposals.hypothesis: Probiotic colonization and host health outcomes in aquaculture systems can be predicted by maintaining key dimensionless parameters (e.g., mixing time constant, biomass-to-volume ratio) analogous to those used in industrial bioreactor scale-up, when moving from tank to pilot-pond trials.
proposals.method: We will first identify critical scale-up parameters in pilot ponds stocked with Nile tilapia, quantifying water flow/turnover (mixing time), stocking density (biomass), and organic load. A PEGylated dihydromyricetin nano-formulation, building on our prior work, will serve as a model antimicrobial/probiotic-enhancing agent. Pilot trials will systematically vary these parameters in a factorial design, measuring probiotic (e.g., Bacillus spp.) colonization via qPCR, pathogen (Aeromonas hydrophila) inhibition, and host outcomes (growth, antioxidant activity, immune response from our established assays). Data will be used to construct a predictive multivariate regression model linking environmental parameters to efficacy endpoints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for designing probiotic trials that account for system scale, moving beyond empirical testing. It will deliver a predictive model to guide probiotic application in diverse aquaculture operations, enhancing reliability and economic viability. The study will also advance the application of nano-encapsulated bioactive compounds in complex aquatic environments.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in adapting established chemical engineering bioreactor scaling principles to the biological complexity of aquaculture, a direct cross-disciplinary analogy not previously applied to probiotic testing. It integrates nano-delivery systems with environmental scaling parameters for predictive modelling.

proposals.title: Phylogenetically-informed probiotic scaling: Predicting cross-species efficacy of functional dietary supplements in aquaculture using host-microbiome interaction mapping
proposals.motivation: The aquaculture industry urgently needs efficient methods to scale the use of functional dietary supplements, like zinc nanoparticles or bioactive compounds, across diverse fish species. Current empirical testing is slow, costly, and lacks a predictive framework. A rational, comparative approach, inspired by drug repurposing in biomedicine, could dramatically accelerate the adoption of proven supplements in new, economically important species like grouper and tilapia.
proposals.hypothesis: The efficacy of a probiotic or functional dietary supplement in a novel fish host can be predicted by the phylogenetic conservation of the specific host mucosal and immune pathways with which it interacts, as well as the core functional profiles of the resident gut microbiome.
proposals.method: We will first perform comparative genomic analysis (using tools like OrthoFinder and IQ-TREE) to construct a phylogeny of host immune and mucosal adhesion genes (e.g., TLRs, mucins) from Nile tilapia (*Oreochromis niloticus*), hybrid grouper, and other key species. Concurrently, we will use 16S rRNA amplicon and shotgun metagenomic sequencing to profile core gut microbiomes and map conserved microbial metabolic pathways (e.g., energy metabolism, oxidative respiration). We will then integrate these datasets to identify conserved host-microbe interaction modules and use this map to predict the efficacy of our lab's existing supplements (e.g., PEGylated dihydromyricetin nanoliposomes, zinc nanoparticles) in phylogenetically proximate species before targeted in vivo validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, predictive framework for rational probiotic and supplement scaling in aquaculture, moving the field beyond trial-and-error. It will generate a publicly available phylogenetic map of host-microbiome interactions for key farmed fish. The validated predictions will directly inform dietary strategies to improve growth performance, antioxidant activity, and immune response in new species.
proposals.novelty: While phylogenetic tools are used in ecology, their application to predict the efficacy of engineered dietary supplements across farmed fish species by integrating host genetics and microbiome functional profiles is novel. This adapts the logic of comparative genomics and drug repurposing to the practical challenges of aquaculture nutrition.

proposals.title: High-throughput screening of probiotic bacterial secretomes reveals host-pathogen-specific antimicrobial efficacy for precision aquaculture
proposals.motivation: Marine aquaculture faces significant losses from bacterial diseases, and overuse of antibiotics drives resistance. Probiotics offer a sustainable alternative, but their efficacy is often inconsistent and host-specific, limiting broad application. A systematic, high-throughput method to deconvolve the specific antimicrobial compounds from probiotics and their spectra against key pathogens across multiple fish species is critically needed to design effective, targeted biocontrols.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that secreted antimicrobials from candidate probiotic bacteria exhibit distinct, quantifiable efficacy patterns against pathogen libraries from different marine fish hosts, and that these patterns can be used to predict and tailor probiotic formulations for specific aquaculture contexts.
proposals.method: We will establish a high-throughput robotic screening platform. Probiotic candidates (e.g., *Lactobacillus*, *Bacillus* spp.) will be cultured, and their cell-free supernatants (secretomes) collected. These will be tested in 96-well microplates against a library of major aquaculture pathogens (e.g., *Vibrio* spp., *Aeromonas* spp.) isolated from Nile tilapia (*Oreochromis niloticus*), groupers, and other species. Growth inhibition will be monitored via automated optical density (OD600) and metabolic activity assays (e.g., resazurin). Data will be analyzed using hierarchical clustering and principal component analysis to map efficacy matrices.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first cross-species efficacy matrix linking probiotic secretions to pathogen susceptibility, moving beyond trial-and-error probiotic selection. It will identify lead antimicrobial compounds for further nano-formulation (informed by Wang's prior work on PEGylated dihydromyricetin and zinc nanoparticles) and provide a rational basis for designing precision dietary supplements that enhance fish health and growth performance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the high-throughput, deconvolutional screening of probiotic secretomes against a multi-host pathogen library, rather than testing whole probiotics in single hosts, to systematically uncover host-pathogen-specific activity spectra for precision aquaculture.

proposals.title: Inverted Core-Shell Zinc Oxide-Polymer Particles for Enhanced Environmental Resilience and Gut-Targeted Delivery of Probiotics in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) Aquaculture
proposals.motivation: Probiotic supplementation in aquaculture is limited by significant environmental degradation (e.g., UV, salinity, microbial competition) during water column transit in open systems, leading to inconsistent delivery and efficacy. Advanced delivery systems are needed to protect probiotics from these pre-ingestion stressors to reliably translate lab-proven benefits in growth, immunity, and disease resistance to commercial-scale pond and net-pen aquaculture.
proposals.hypothesis: An inverted core-shell delivery system, with probiotics in a polymer core and ZnO nanoparticles in a protective shell, will significantly enhance probiotic viability during environmental exposure and improve growth performance, antioxidant activity, and immune response in Nile tilapia compared to conventional probiotic delivery.
proposals.method: We will synthesize core-shell particles via a water-in-oil-in-water double emulsion-solvent evaporation technique, encapsulating a commercial *Bacillus* sp. probiotic within a poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) core. The shell will be composed of a zein matrix with embedded ZnO nanoparticles synthesized via a green method using dihydromyricetin (inspired by Wang's prior work). We will characterize particles (size, encapsulation efficiency, ZnO release) and test UV/shielding and antimicrobial properties in vitro. An 8-week pond trial with Nile tilapia will compare: 1) control diet, 2) diet with free probiotics, 3) diet with core-shell particles. We will measure standard zootechnical parameters, tissue mineralization, serum antioxidant enzymes (SOD, CAT), and immune markers (lysozyme, respiratory burst activity).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel material solution to the environmental delivery bottleneck in aquaculture probiotics. It will generate critical *in vivo* data linking advanced delivery system design to measurable improvements in fish health and productivity under realistic farming conditions, moving beyond lab-based efficacy studies.
proposals.novelty: The inverted core-shell architecture (probiotic core, functional ZnO shell) is novel for aquaculture, specifically designed for environmental resilience rather than just gut release. It uniquely integrates Wang's expertise in zinc nanoparticle synthesis and dietary application for fish with a controlled-release polymer system for biotic payloads.

proposals.title: Dietary Modulation of Gut Microenvironment Dictates the Biodistribution and Synergistic Efficacy of Lactobacillus rhamnosus-Functionalized ZnO/Dihydromyricetin Nanohybrids in a Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) Model
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows diet composition profoundly shapes gut physiology, mucus, and microbiota, which are critical barriers and targets for oral nanotherapeutics and probiotics. However, the in vivo fate and combined efficacy of diet, nano-delivery systems, and live biotherapeutics are rarely studied as an integrated system, creating a knowledge gap for designing robust oral treatments. This is particularly relevant for aquaculture, where dietary formulations are routinely manipulated for growth and health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that basal diet (high-fat, high-fiber, or standard) will alter the intestinal microenvironment, thereby controlling the mucosal adhesion, spatial distribution, and drug release kinetics of probiotic-coated ZnO nanocarriers, ultimately determining the synergistic outcome on intestinal health and barrier function.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize PEG/tea saponin-coated ZnO nanoparticles loaded with dihydromyricetin (DMY), then conjugate them with Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG via EDC/NHS chemistry. Nile tilapia will be fed isocaloric diets (high-lipid, high-cellulose/cornstarch, standard) for 4 weeks before oral gavage with Cy7-labeled nanohybrids. In vivo fluorescence imaging will track biodistribution. Fish will be sacrificed to analyze gut pH, mucus thickness, nanohybrid localization (CLSM), probiotic colonization (qPCR), and therapeutic markers (antioxidant activity, immune gene expression, histopathology).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a causal link between macronutrient diet and the performance of a complex probiotic-nanohybrid delivery system in vivo. It will provide a framework for 'diet-aware' nanomedicine design and identify specific dietary contexts that either enhance or hinder synergistic efficacy, offering practical guidance for nutritional management during nanotherapeutic interventions in animal health.
proposals.novelty: While diet-nanoparticle or diet-probiotic interactions have been studied separately, the tripartite interaction between a defined diet, a drug-loaded inorganic nanocarrier, and a physically conjugated probiotic strain is unexplored. The use of a functionalized ZnO/DMY nanohybrid, building on Wang's prior work with zinc supplements and nano-formulations, to probe this interaction is novel.

proposals.title: Dietary Protein Overload Antagonizes Fenofibrate Efficacy: A Study on Growth, Metabolic Stress, and Hepato-renal Health in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus)
proposals.motivation: In aquaculture, high-protein diets are widely used to promote fish growth, but their long-term metabolic consequences, especially when combined with lipid-lowering agents like fenofibrate, are poorly understood. Pre-2026 research indicates fenofibrate improves lipid metabolism in fish, yet protein excess can induce metabolic stress and oxidative damage, potentially negating such benefits. This study addresses the critical gap in understanding nutrient-drug interactions in teleost nutrition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an excessively high dietary protein level will induce metabolic and oxidative stress, thereby negating the beneficial effects of fenofibrate on lipid metabolism and causing new adverse effects on liver and kidney function in Nile tilapia.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 10-week feeding trial with juvenile Nile tilapia using a 2x3 factorial design: two fenofibrate levels (0 vs. supplemented) and three dietary protein levels (optimal, high, and excessively high). Growth performance, feed utilization, and tissue (liver, kidney, muscle) samples will be collected. We will analyze plasma biochemical parameters (lipids, ALT, AST, creatinine), tissue antioxidant activity (SOD, CAT, MDA), and immune response (lysozyme, TNF-α). Histopathological examination of liver and kidney will assess tissue damage. Data will be analyzed using two-way ANOVA.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical evidence on the antagonistic interaction between dietary protein overload and fenofibrate in fish, defining a practical upper limit for protein in medicated feeds. It will contribute a nuanced understanding of nutrient-drug interactions to the field of aquaculture nutrition, informing more precise and sustainable feed formulations.
proposals.novelty: While protein effects and fenofibrate benefits are studied in isolation, their interaction is unexplored. The novelty lies in testing the non-obvious premise that a typically beneficial macronutrient (protein) can negate pharmaceutical efficacy and become detrimental under specific dietary conditions.

proposals.title: Hormetic Effects of Dietary Protein Restriction: Evaluating the Adaptive Role of Induced Oxidative Stress on Growth Performance and Stress Resilience in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus)
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 aquaculture research predominantly views oxidative stress as a pathological state to be mitigated, especially under nutritional challenges like low-protein diets. However, emerging concepts in biology suggest low-level oxidative stress can trigger beneficial hormetic adaptations, enhancing cellular defense and metabolic efficiency. This paradigm shift remains unexplored in farmed fish, where optimizing dietary protein is crucial for sustainability and cost.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a moderately low-protein diet will induce a sub-lethal, hormetic level of oxidative stress in Nile tilapia, leading to an adaptive upregulation of antioxidant and metabolic pathways that ultimately improves growth efficiency, stress tolerance, and immune function compared to fish on either a standard or severely deficient protein diet.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled feeding trial with juvenile Nile tilapia assigned to three isoenergetic diets: optimal protein (control), moderately low protein (MLP), and severely low protein (SLP). Using methods from Wang's recent work, we will synthesize and characterize zinc-dihydromyricetin nanoparticles (Zn-DMY NPs) as a novel, traceable pro-oxidant challenge agent to quantify stress resilience post-dietary conditioning. Growth performance, tissue mineralization, and energy metabolism will be assessed. Oxidative stress markers (ROS, MDA), antioxidant enzyme activities (SOD, CAT, GPx), and key immune parameters (lysozyme, complement activity) will be measured in liver and serum at multiple timepoints to map the adaptive response trajectory.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence for a hormetic benefit of oxidative stress in a teleost nutrition model, challenging the prevailing 'stress-as-damage' view. It will establish a methodological framework using bioactive nanoparticles to probe stress resilience. The findings could redefine nutritional strategies, advocating for controlled dietary stress to enhance fish robustness and reduce reliance on high-protein feeds.
proposals.novelty: The proposal uniquely integrates the concept of hormesis from gerontology and toxicology into practical aquaculture nutrition. It innovatively employs Zn-DMY NPs, previously studied for antimicrobial and growth-promoting effects, as a tool to actively test the adaptive capacity conferred by a low-protein diet, moving beyond correlation to causation.

proposals.title: Long-term dietary fenofibrate supplementation in Nile tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus): A comprehensive assessment of growth performance, lipid metabolism, immune-antioxidant status, and economic viability over a full production cycle.
proposals.motivation: In aquaculture, optimizing feed efficiency and fish health is critical for economic and environmental sustainability. Fenofibrate, a PPARα agonist, is known to modulate lipid metabolism in mammals and has shown short-term promise in improving lipid utilization in fish. However, its long-term effects on comprehensive health metrics, disease resilience, and overall farm economics over an entire grow-out period remain uncharacterized, creating a significant knowledge gap for practical application.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that long-term, low-dose dietary fenofibrate supplementation will enhance growth performance and feed efficiency in Nile tilapia by promoting lipid catabolism for energy, while simultaneously improving immune function and antioxidant capacity, leading to a net positive economic outcome.
proposals.method: Nile tilapia fingerlings will be reared in a controlled recirculating aquaculture system and fed isonitrogenous and isoenergetic diets supplemented with graded levels of fenofibrate (0, 50, 100, 200 mg/kg diet) for a full 16-week production cycle. Growth performance (weight gain, FCR, SGR) will be monitored bi-weekly. At termination, tissue samples will be analyzed for lipid content and fatty acid profiles. Key health indicators will be assessed, including serum lipid profiles, liver PPARα gene expression, activities of antioxidant enzymes (SOD, CAT, GPx), and immune parameters (lysozyme, respiratory burst). An economic analysis will calculate the benefit-cost ratio based on feed costs, growth data, and simulated survival rates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first holistic, production-cycle-long dataset on fenofibrate's application in tilapia aquaculture, directly linking physiological mechanisms to practical outcomes. It will establish optimal dosage guidelines and deliver a clear economic model to assess its viability for commercial adoption, moving beyond proof-of-concept to practical recommendation.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have examined short-term metabolic effects of fibrates, this work uniquely integrates long-term zootechnical, physiological, and economic analyses within a real-world aquaculture production framework, focusing on the sustainable use of a repurposed pharmaceutical as a functional feed additive.

proposals.title: Fenofibrate and Dietary Lipid Modulation in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus): Linking Gut Microbiota Dysbiosis to Hepatic Metabolic and Oxidative Stress Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Fenofibrate, a peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor alpha (PPARα) agonist, is used to study lipid metabolism in fish models, but its impact on the gut microbiome remains poorly characterized. Given the established pre-2026 knowledge that gut microbiota significantly influences host metabolism, antioxidant status, and immunity in aquaculture species, understanding how pharmaceutical and dietary interventions jointly alter microbial ecology is crucial for sustainable fish health and nutrition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fenofibrate administration, combined with different dietary lipid sources, will induce specific shifts in the gut microbial community structure of Nile tilapia, and that these dysbiotic changes will correlate directly with alterations in hepatic lipid metabolism, oxidative stress markers, and immune parameters.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 10-week feeding trial with Nile tilapia using a 2x2 factorial design: two diets (conventional vs. a novel lipid source like structured lipid or algal oil) and with/without fenofibrate supplementation. Gut microbiota will be analyzed via 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing (V3-V4 region) on the Illumina MiSeq platform. Data will be processed using QIIME2 (pre-2026 version) for taxonomic assignment and alpha/beta diversity analysis. Microbial community shifts will be statistically linked (using Spearman correlation and redundancy analysis in R) to host response data: growth performance, tissue mineralization, hepatic PPARα expression, antioxidant enzyme activity (SOD, CAT), and immune markers (lysozyme, TNF-α).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive link between fenofibrate-induced gut microbiota changes and metabolic phenotypes in a teleost model. It will clarify the role of the gut-liver axis in pharmaceutical nutrient interactions, offering insights for designing functional feeds that mitigate drug side effects and improve fish health through microbiome management.
proposals.novelty: While Wang's prior work examined zinc nanoparticles and dietary supplements on fish physiology, this proposal uniquely integrates a pharmaceutical lipid modulator with dietary intervention to mechanistically dissect the gut microbiome's role as a mediator of metabolic and oxidative stress outcomes, a novel synthesis in aquaculture research.

proposals.title: Quantifying Fenofibrate-Induced Metabolic Flux Alterations in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) Using Stable Isotope Tracer Analysis
proposals.motivation: Fenofibrate, a PPARα agonist, is a known lipid-modulating drug in mammals, but its real-time, systemic effects on lipid and protein metabolism in teleost fish remain poorly quantified. Understanding these metabolic fluxes is critical for aquaculture, as optimizing lipid utilization can significantly improve feed efficiency and fish health. Pre-2026, metabolic flux analysis (MFA) using isotopic tracers is well-established in mammalian systems but underutilized in fish nutrition research, creating a knowledge gap in precise nutrient partitioning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dietary fenofibrate supplementation will significantly redirect central carbon flux in Nile tilapia, increasing the fractional contribution of labeled precursors (e.g., [U-13C]glucose) into de novo lipogenesis while simultaneously decreasing the flux into protein synthesis pathways.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled feeding trial with Nile tilapia, using diets supplemented with fenofibrate versus a control. After an acclimation period, fish will receive a pulse of [U-13C]glucose via intraperitoneal injection. Tissue samples (liver, muscle, white adipose) will be collected at serial time points. Metabolite extraction and GC-MS analysis will be performed to measure 13C enrichment in key intermediates (e.g., acetyl-CoA, TCA cycle intermediates, amino acids). Flux distributions will be quantified using computational MFA, specifically employing the COBRA (Constraint-Based Reconstruction and Analysis) toolbox in MATLAB to create a stoichiometric network model of core metabolism and fit it to the isotopic labeling data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative, system-level map of fenofibrate's action on in vivo metabolic fluxes in a commercially important fish species. It will directly link dietary intervention to specific pathway activities, offering a mechanistic explanation for changes in growth performance and body composition. The work will establish a novel MFA pipeline for aquaculture research, moving beyond static biomarker measurements.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct application of advanced mammalian metabolic flux analysis techniques to a fish model for pharmaceutical-nutrient interaction, combined with the researcher's existing expertise in nano-delivery (e.g., PEGylated nanoliposomes) and zinc metabolism in tilapia, suggesting potential future work on targeted delivery of metabolic modulators.

proposals.title: Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic Modeling of Fenofibrate in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus): Elucidating the Role of Dietary Protein on Lipid Metabolism Modulation
proposals.motivation: Fenofibrate, a peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor alpha (PPARα) agonist, shows promise as a lipid-lowering agent in aquaculture to improve fish health and product quality. However, its efficacy is highly variable, and pre-2026 studies in mammals indicate that dietary macronutrients, especially protein, can significantly alter drug absorption and metabolism. A mechanistic understanding of this interaction in fish is lacking, hindering the development of precise, effective dietary-pharmaceutical regimens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dietary protein level significantly alters the pharmacokinetics (PK) of fenofibrate in Nile tilapia, and that a developed PK-pharmacodynamic (PD) model will quantitatively link these changes to differential effects on lipid metabolism and growth performance.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled feeding trial with tilapia fed isoenergetic diets varying in protein level. Following fenofibrate administration, serial blood and tissue sampling will establish concentration-time profiles. Data will be fitted using non-linear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM) to build a multi-compartment PK model. This PK model will be linked to a PD model using an indirect response framework, where fenofibrate concentration drives the inhibition of a lipid synthesis or mobilization rate constant, with model parameters (e.g., clearance, volume of distribution) explicitly tested for dependency on dietary protein intake.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first quantitative PK-PD model for fenofibrate in a teleost fish, directly linking diet to drug action. It will provide a predictive tool for optimizing fenofibrate dosing based on feed composition and establish a novel methodology for applying advanced pharmacological modeling from human medicine to aquaculture nutrition and health.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of human pharmacokinetic modeling techniques (NONMEM, indirect response PD models) to aquaculture, and the explicit integration of a major dietary variable (protein) as a covariate directly into the drug model's parameters, moving beyond empirical observation to mechanistic prediction.

proposals.title: Trophic Transfer of Fenofibrate-Induced Metabolic Effects in a Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) – African Sharptooth Catfish (Clarias gariepinus) Food Chain
proposals.motivation: Pharmaceutical pollutants like the lipid regulator fenofibrate are increasingly detected in aquatic ecosystems, posing a significant ecotoxicological threat. While direct effects on single species are documented, pre-2026 knowledge lacks empirical evidence on whether such metabolic disruptors can scale across trophic levels, potentially amplifying impacts through aquatic food webs. This gap hinders accurate ecological risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dietary exposure of prey fish (Nile tilapia) to environmentally relevant concentrations of fenofibrate will induce significant metabolic and oxidative stress responses that are transferred to and manifested in a predator fish (African sharptooth catfish) through trophic consumption, altering predator energy metabolism and antioxidant activity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled mesocosm study using a two-trophic-level model. Nile tilapia fry will be fed one of three diets: control, low-dose (1 µg/g), or high-dose (10 µg/g) fenofibrate-supplemented feed for 8 weeks. Subsequently, naive catfish will be fed exclusively on these pre-exposed tilapia for 4 weeks. Growth performance, tissue fenofibrate residues (via LC-MS/MS), key metabolic enzyme activities (e.g., CPT-1, ACOX1), and systemic oxidative stress markers (SOD, CAT, MDA) will be quantified in both prey and predator. Data will be analyzed using multivariate ANOVA and structural equation modeling to trace effect pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental evidence for the trophic transfer of fenofibrate's physiological effects in a fish food chain. It will establish a mechanistic link between prey exposure and predator metabolic phenotype, directly contributing to the fields of ecotoxicology and food web ecology. The findings will inform regulatory frameworks on the ecological risks of pharmaceutical contaminants.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond standard single-species bioassays by explicitly testing the transfer of a metabolic disruptor's effects through predation, a non-obvious pathway given fenofibrate's presumed rapid metabolism.
