It uniquely integrates Wang's expertise in dietary supplements, fish physiology, and nanomaterial-based inhibition of energy metabolism to investigate a contaminant-driven ecological process.

proposals.title: Comparative Efficacy of Alternative Amino Acid-Chelated Copper on Growth, Antioxidant Status, and Mineral Bioavailability in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus)
proposals.motivation: Methionine is the industry-standard amino acid for trace mineral chelation in aquafeeds, primarily for its dual role as a chelator and an essential amino acid. However, its high cost and variable market availability drive the need to evaluate cheaper, equally effective alternatives. This research directly addresses aquafeed sustainability by investigating whether other amino acids (e.g., lysine, histidine) or small peptides can form bioavailable copper chelates with potentially unique physiological benefits, such as enhanced antioxidant or immune modulation, beyond mere mineral delivery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that copper chelated with amino acids other than methionine (specifically lysine and histidine) will provide equivalent or superior bioavailability in Nile tilapia, as measured by tissue mineral deposition, and will induce distinct, beneficial shifts in antioxidant activity and immune parameters compared to conventional copper methionine and inorganic copper sulfate.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize copper chelates with L-lysine and L-histidine using established methods (e.g., aqueous complexation, spray-drying). A 10-week feeding trial will be conducted with juvenile Nile tilapia (initial weight ~10g) in a recirculating system. Six isonitrogenous and isocaloric diets will be formulated: a negative control (basal diet with minimal Cu), a positive control with CuSO4, a control with commercial Cu-methionine, and three experimental diets with equimolar copper from the novel lysine, histidine, and a lysine-histidine mixed-ligand chelate. Growth performance (FCR, SGR), tissue (liver, vertebrae) copper and zinc mineralization, serum antioxidant enzymes (SOD, CAT), and immune markers (lysozyme, complement activity) will be analyzed. Data will be evaluated using one-way ANOVA followed by Duncan's multiple range test (p<0.05).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for the viability of non-methionine copper chelates in practical aquaculture nutrition, potentially reducing feed costs. It will expand the understanding of how chelating ligand identity influences not only mineral absorption but also systemic antioxidant and immune responses in fish, moving beyond simple bioavailability metrics.
proposals.novelty: While amino acid chelates are common, directly challenging the necessity of methionine for copper chelation in vivo using a comprehensive physiological and biochemical framework is novel. The investigation of histidine, a known copper-binding amino acid in physiological systems, as a deliberate feed-grade chelator, and its potential to modulate antioxidant pathways distinct from methionine, represents a significant departure from standard industry practice.

proposals.title: Investigating the Growth-Immunity Trade-off: Dietary NCM-Cu Supplementation in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) Under Disease-Free Conditions
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Wang's work, demonstrates that novel copper supplements like nano-complexes (NCM-Cu) enhance immunity and disease resistance in aquaculture. However, the prevailing assumption is that improved immunity inherently supports better growth. This overlooks the potential metabolic cost of immune system priming, which could divert energy from somatic growth under optimal, pathogen-free conditions where such a robust immune response is not needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dietary supplementation with NCM-Cu, while enhancing immune markers, will induce a measurable trade-off, resulting in reduced growth performance and feed utilization in Nile tilapia reared under strictly controlled, disease-free conditions compared to fish fed a diet with a conventional copper source.
proposals.method: A controlled feeding trial will be conducted with juvenile Nile tilapia. Fish will be randomly assigned to one of three isonitrogenous and isoenergetic diets: a control diet with CuSO4, a diet with NCM-Cu at an immunostimulatory dose (based on pre-2026 titration studies), and a pair-fed group receiving the control diet but ration-matched to the NCM-Cu group's intake. Key metrics include specific growth rate (SGR), feed conversion ratio (FCR), and tissue mineralization. Immune status will be quantified via lysozyme activity, respiratory burst, and expression of key immune genes (e.g., IL-1β, TNF-α). Energy metabolism will be assessed through liver glycolytic enzyme activity and whole-body proximate composition.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide critical empirical data on the potential growth penalty of prophylactic immunostimulation, challenging the simplistic 'more immunity equals better performance' paradigm. It will offer a framework for defining a cost-benefit ratio for novel mineral supplements in aquaculture, guiding more precise nutritional strategies that balance health and productivity.
proposals.novelty: While most studies test novel supplements against disease challenges, this work uniquely isolates the metabolic cost of immune priming by using a stringent disease-free environment and a pair-feeding design, directly testing the trade-off theory often discussed but rarely measured in aquaculture nutrition.

proposals.title: Disentangling Antioxidant and Mineral Delivery: The Role of Methionine in Mediating the Physiological Effects of Dietary Copper in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus)
proposals.motivation: In aquaculture nutrition, copper (Cu) is an essential trace mineral often supplemented in diets, with its benefits attributed to its role as a cofactor for antioxidant enzymes like superoxide dismutase. However, Cu is commonly provided as Cu-methionine, a chelate where the methionine ligand itself possesses potent antioxidant properties. This confounding factor makes it unclear whether observed improvements in growth, antioxidant status, and immunity in fish are due to Cu delivery or the ancillary antioxidant action of methionine, limiting precise nutrient formulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the primary mechanism behind the beneficial effects of dietary Cu-methionine supplementation in Nile tilapia is the antioxidant activity of the methionine ligand, not the delivery and utilization of copper ions for enzymatic function.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 10-week feeding trial with juvenile Nile tilapia using six isonitrogenous and isoenergetic diets: a Cu-unsupplemented basal diet, diets supplemented with inorganic copper sulfate (CuSO4), organic Cu-methionine (Cu-Met), equimolar levels of inorganic copper plus free methionine (CuSO4 + Met), free methionine alone (Met), and an inert zinc oxide (ZnO) control. Growth performance, tissue (liver, muscle) copper and zinc mineralization, and key physiological responses will be analyzed. Specifically, we will measure liver antioxidant enzyme activities (SOD, CAT, GPx), lipid peroxidation (MDA levels), and immune parameters (lysozyme activity, respiratory burst). Gene expression of antioxidant (sod, cat, gpx) and metallothionein (mt) genes will be quantified via qPCR. A metabolomic analysis (using pre-2026 LC-MS platforms) of liver tissue will profile changes in sulfur-amino acid and glutathione metabolism pathways.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct evidence separating the antioxidant contribution of methionine from the trace mineral function of copper in aquafeeds. It will clarify the primary mode of action of a widely used supplement, leading to more efficient and cost-effective diet formulations. The results will challenge the conventional paradigm of mineral nutrition by highlighting the significant role of organic ligand biochemistry.
proposals.novelty: While Cu-methionine's efficacy is established, systematically isolating the effects of its components against matched controls (CuSO4+Met, Met alone) is novel. This approach directly tests the core assumption of copper's primary role, leveraging the researcher's expertise in mineral nutrition, antioxidant assessment, and advanced analytical methods from recent work.

proposals.title: Dietary Supplementation with Nano-Chelated Mineral Copper (NCM-Cu) Enhances Disease Resistance in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) Against Streptococcus agalactiae and Aeromonas hydrophila
proposals.motivation: Bacterial diseases like streptococcosis and motile Aeromonas septicemia cause severe economic losses in tilapia aquaculture. Pre-2026 research by Wang et al. demonstrates the success of nano-delivery systems (e.g., zinc nanoparticles, PEGylated nanoliposomes) in enhancing mineral bioavailability and antimicrobial activity in fish diets, but the specific efficacy of a novel nano-chelated copper (NCM-Cu) supplement against key pathogens remains untested.
proposals.hypothesis: Dietary supplementation with NCM-Cu will significantly improve the survival rate, modulate immune parameters, and reduce bacterial load in Nile tilapia experimentally challenged with Streptococcus agalactiae and Aeromonas hydrophila, compared to fish fed a conventional copper supplement or an unsupplemented control diet.
proposals.method: We will synthesize NCM-Cu using a modified bio-conditioning method analogous to Wang's prior poly-dihydromyricetin zinc nanoparticle synthesis, chelating copper with a biocompatible organic ligand. A 60-day feeding trial will be conducted with three dietary groups: a basal diet (control), a diet supplemented with conventional copper sulfate, and a diet supplemented with NCM-Cu. Following the feeding period, fish from each group will be intraperitoneally challenged with standardized doses of S. agalactiae or A. hydrophila. Key metrics will include cumulative survival, clinical signs, post-mortem bacterial re-isolation from spleen and kidney, and analysis of immune-related gene expression (e.g., IL-1β, TNF-α, lysozyme) and antioxidant activity in serum and tissues.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical data on the in vivo protective efficacy of NCM-Cu against major bacterial pathogens in tilapia. It will directly contribute to the field of aquafeeds by validating a novel nano-mineral delivery system for disease prevention, potentially reducing antibiotic use. The findings will also elucidate the mechanism of action, linking enhanced mineral delivery to improved innate immune and antioxidant responses.
proposals.novelty: While nano-zinc and encapsulated flavonoids have been studied, the application of a specifically designed nano-chelated copper form for targeted disease resistance in tilapia is novel. The study uniquely integrates Wang's expertise in nanomaterial synthesis for aquaculture, dietary conditioning, and pathogen challenge models to test a holistic health outcome.

proposals.title: Dose-Response and Temporal Efficacy of Nano-Chelated Mineral Copper (NCM-Cu) on Antioxidant Status, Immune Function, and Tissue Health in Juvenile Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus)
proposals.motivation: In aquaculture, copper is an essential micronutrient for immune and antioxidant functions, but its inorganic forms have low bioavailability and potential toxicity. Pre-2026 research, including Wang's work on zinc nanoparticles and gluconic acid chelates, demonstrates the efficacy of advanced mineral delivery systems in fish. Establishing precise dosage and duration for novel nano-chelated copper (NCM-Cu) is critical to maximize health benefits and growth performance in economically vital species like Nile tilapia, while ensuring safety and sustainable practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dietary NCM-Cu supplementation will exhibit a defined dose- and time-dependent enhancement of antioxidant capacity and immune response in Nile tilapia, with an optimal range that provides maximal benefit without inducing oxidative stress or histopathological damage in key tissues.
proposals.method: A 10-week feeding trial will be conducted with juvenile Nile tilapia (initial weight ~10g) in a recirculating aquaculture system. A basal diet will be supplemented with NCM-Cu at four graded levels (0, 2, 4, 8 mg Cu/kg diet). Fish will be sampled at weeks 4, 7, and 10. Response variables will include: growth performance (SGR, FCR); tissue (liver, kidney) copper accumulation via ICP-MS; antioxidant enzyme activities (SOD, CAT, GPx) and lipid peroxidation (MDA) in serum and liver; innate immune parameters (lysozyme, complement activity, respiratory burst); and histopathology of liver and intestine. Dose-response curves will be modeled using non-linear regression (sigmoidal Emax model) to determine effective (ED50) and optimal doses.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive dose-duration matrix for NCM-Cu in tilapia, defining the therapeutic window for immune-antioxidant benefits. It will contribute a practical dietary formulation guideline for aquafeed and advance the understanding of mineral nano-chelates' pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics in aquatic species, bridging the gap between novel supplement development and practical application.
proposals.novelty: While previous work by Wang and others focused on zinc nanoparticles and organic acids, this proposal systematically investigates the under-explored copper nano-chelate, uniquely integrating temporal sampling with multi-tissue biomarker analysis to delineate both the efficacy and safety thresholds, a non-obvious progression from existing mineral supplementation studies.

proposals.title: Dietary Nano-Cu-Montmorillonite Supplementation Enhances Fillet Quality, Shelf-Life, and Nutritional Profile in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus)
proposals.motivation: Consumer demand for high-quality, nutritious, and long-lasting fish fillets is driving aquaculture research beyond growth performance to post-harvest quality. Pre-2026 studies show that mineral nanoparticles, like zinc, can improve fish health and fillet attributes, but the specific effects of novel copper-based nanoclays (NCM-Cu) on fillet texture, oxidative stability, and nutrient retention remain unexplored. This gap limits the development of targeted dietary strategies to directly enhance product value and market appeal.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dietary supplementation with NCM-Cu will improve the texture, extend the shelf-life by reducing lipid oxidation, and enhance the nutritional profile (e.g., mineral content, fatty acid composition) of Nile tilapia fillets compared to conventional copper sources or unsupplemented diets.
proposals.method: Nile tilapia will be fed diets supplemented with NCM-Cu at graded levels (0, 50, 100, 200 mg/kg diet) in a 60-day feeding trial, using cornstarch-based diets as a carrier. Fillet quality will be assessed post-harvest using texture profile analysis (TPA) for hardness and chewiness, thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) assay for lipid oxidation during refrigerated storage, and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) for mineral profiling. Fatty acid composition will be analyzed via gas chromatography, and antioxidant activity in fillet tissue will be measured via superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase (CAT) assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct link between a novel nano-mineral supplement and key consumer-oriented fillet quality parameters, moving beyond growth metrics. It will provide concrete data on the optimal dietary level of NCM-Cu for quality enhancement, offering a practical dietary strategy for tilapia producers. The findings will contribute to the emerging field of nanotechnology in aquaculture by demonstrating its application for post-harvest product improvement.
proposals.novelty: While Wang's prior work focused on zinc nanoparticles and their effects on growth and immunity, this proposal uniquely targets a different nanomaterial (Cu-montmorillonite) for the specific, underexplored purpose of modulating post-mortem fillet characteristics. It integrates nanomaterial science with food quality assessment, a novel intersection in aquaculture nutrition research pre-2026.

proposals.title: Dietary Copper Chelation in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus): Impacts on Growth, Antioxidant Defense, and Immune Function
proposals.motivation: While extensive research, including Dr. Wang's prior work, focuses on the benefits of supplementing trace minerals like zinc, the physiological consequences of specific micronutrient deficiencies in aquaculture remain less explored. Copper is a critical cofactor for enzymes involved in immunity, oxidative metabolism, and iron utilization. A systematic study of induced copper deficiency is essential to understand its specific pathology and establish precise dietary requirements for optimal fish health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dietary copper chelation using a specific agent (e.g., bathocuproine disulfonate) will induce a quantifiable deficiency state in Nile tilapia, leading to impaired growth performance, reduced activity of copper-dependent enzymes (e.g., superoxide dismutase, cytochrome c oxidase), and compromised innate immune response.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled feeding trial with juvenile Nile tilapia. A basal diet will be formulated using cornstarch and casein as primary ingredients, with a copper-chelating agent added to the experimental group. Growth performance (weight gain, feed conversion ratio) will be monitored. Post-trial, we will analyze tissue (liver, gill) copper levels, measure the activity of key copper-dependent enzymes (SOD1, COX), and assess immune parameters including lysozyme activity, respiratory burst of head-kidney macrophages, and susceptibility to a controlled bacterial challenge (e.g., Aeromonas hydrophila).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a detailed physiological profile of copper deficiency in a key aquaculture species, defining specific biomarkers (enzymatic, immune) for deficiency diagnosis. It will contribute critical data for refining dietary copper requirements and offer a comparative model to understand the distinct roles of copper versus other trace minerals like zinc in piscine health.
proposals.novelty: This work inverts the dominant supplementation paradigm to study deficiency, and uniquely applies a targeted chelation approach (versus simple omission) to create a more specific deficiency model in fish, allowing for clearer causal links between copper status and physiological outcomes.

proposals.title: Optimizing Nano-Chelated Mineral Copper (NCM-Cu) Formulation to Maximize Retention and Minimize Effluent Excretion in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus): A Dual-Objective Nutritional Strategy
proposals.motivation: Copper is an essential trace mineral in aquaculture feeds, but traditional supplements like copper sulfate exhibit low bioavailability, leading to significant excretion and environmental pollution in effluent water. Pre-2026 research in nanodelivery systems, such as zinc nanoparticles and PEGylated nanoliposomes, demonstrates enhanced mineral bioavailability and retention in fish, yet a targeted strategy to simultaneously optimize nutrient retention and minimize aquatic waste for copper remains underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specifically engineered Nano-Chelated Mineral Copper (NCM-Cu), synthesized using a bio-conditioning method with a dietary polymer like cornstarch or PEG, will significantly improve copper retention in Nile tilapia tissues while drastically reducing its concentration in rearing water compared to conventional copper supplements.
proposals.method: We will synthesize NCM-Cu via a green, bio-conditioned method using dihydromyricetin (DMY) as a chelating and reducing agent, analogous to Wang's prior work with zinc, to create stable, poly-DMY-coated copper nanoparticles. A feeding trial will be conducted with Nile tilapia using diets supplemented with: 1) conventional CuSO4, 2) low-dose NCM-Cu, and 3) high-dose NCM-Cu. Growth performance, tissue (liver, muscle) copper concentration, antioxidant activity (SOD, CAT), and immune response (lysozyme, respiratory burst) will be measured. Crucially, copper concentration in tank effluent will be monitored daily using ICP-MS. A dual-objective optimization algorithm (e.g., a Pareto front analysis using response surface methodology) will identify the formulation that maximizes tissue retention metrics while minimizing effluent copper.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, environmentally responsible nutritional strategy for copper supplementation in aquaculture, directly addressing effluent pollution. It will contribute a validated NCM-Cu formulation and a dual-objective optimization framework that can be adapted for other trace minerals, advancing the field of sustainable aquafeed development.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit dual-objective optimization (fish health vs. environmental impact) of a nanomineral supplement, moving beyond simply measuring bioavailability to actively minimizing waste, and in applying Wang's established bio-conditioned nanoparticle synthesis platform specifically to the problem of copper pollution.

proposals.title: Evaluating the interactive effects of high cornstarch diets and elevated water temperature on growth, metabolic stress, and immune function in Nile tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus)
proposals.motivation: High dietary carbohydrates are often considered detrimental in aquaculture, linked to metabolic disorders and reduced health, especially in carnivorous fish. However, the context-dependency of this effect, particularly under climate change-induced warmer water conditions which increase metabolic rates, is poorly understood. This study addresses a critical gap in sustainable aquaculture feed formulation by testing if the negative impacts of high-carbohydrate diets are universal or contingent on environmental stressors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the detrimental effects of high cornstarch diets on Nile tilapia will be significantly exacerbated under elevated water temperatures, manifesting as impaired growth performance, heightened oxidative stress, and suppressed immune response compared to fish reared at optimal temperatures.
proposals.method: A 2x2 factorial design will be employed, with Nile tilapia juveniles fed either a conventional (control) diet or a high cornstarch diet (35% inclusion), each reared at two water temperatures: optimal (28°C) and elevated (32°C). The 8-week growth trial will measure standard performance metrics (weight gain, FCR). Post-trial, tissue samples (liver, muscle, serum) will be analyzed for metabolic markers (glycogen, glucose), antioxidant activity (SOD, CAT, MDA), and immune parameters (lysozyme, respiratory burst). Statistical analysis will use two-way ANOVA to identify main and interactive effects of diet and temperature.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a nuanced, condition-specific assessment of carbohydrate utilization in a key aquaculture species, directly informing feed strategies for varying thermal environments. It will generate novel data on the physiological interplay between dietary energy source and thermal stress, contributing to more resilient and efficient aquaculture practices in a warming climate.
proposals.novelty: While high-carbohydrate diets have been studied, their interaction with a key climate-related husbandry factor (chronic elevated temperature) is novel. The proposal uniquely integrates Dr. Wang's expertise in fish nutrition, zinc/antioxidant biology, and nanomaterial-based delivery systems, positioning him to later explore targeted interventions (e.g., zinc nanoparticles) based on the stress pathways identified.

proposals.title: Optimizing Dietary Protein-to-Energy Ratios for Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus): A Response Surface Approach to Simultaneously Vary Protein and Cornstarch Levels
proposals.motivation: In aquaculture, formulating cost-effective and sustainable diets is critical. Traditional experimental designs often hold protein constant while varying energy sources, which fails to capture the interactive effects of macronutrients on fish growth and health. This limits the ability to define optimal protein-to-energy ratios, a key factor for maximizing feed efficiency and minimizing nitrogen waste in Nile tilapia farming.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that simultaneously varying dietary protein and cornstarch (a primary energy source) will reveal a significant interactive effect on growth performance, nutrient utilization, and physiological status in juvenile Nile tilapia, leading to the identification of a non-linear optimal dietary ratio.
proposals.method: A central composite rotatable design (Response Surface Methodology, RSM) will be employed to formulate nine isocaloric experimental diets with varying levels of crude protein (28-38%) and cornstarch (20-40%). Juvenile Nile tilapia will be fed these diets for 8 weeks. Growth performance (weight gain, FCR), tissue mineralization (vertebrae ash), antioxidant activity (SOD, CAT in liver), and immune response (lysozyme, respiratory burst activity) will be measured. Data will be analyzed using RSM to model responses and identify optimal regions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a precise, multidimensional model for protein and energy requirements in Nile tilapia, moving beyond single-nutrient studies. It will directly contribute to more efficient feed formulations that support growth while enhancing antioxidant and immune capacity, potentially reducing disease susceptibility and environmental impact.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying RSM to deconstruct the fixed-protein paradigm in fish nutrition experiments, explicitly testing for protein-energy interactions using cornstarch, and linking these macronutrient ratios to integrative physiological responses (growth, mineralization, antioxidant, immune) based on the researcher's established expertise.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Optimal Dietary Cornstarch Threshold: Effects on Growth Performance, Antioxidant Status, and Immune Response in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus)
proposals.motivation: Cornstarch is a common, low-cost carbohydrate source in aquafeeds, but excessive levels can impair fish health and growth. Prior studies, including Wang's own work on dietary zinc supplements for tilapia, indicate that both deficient and excessive carbohydrate levels are detrimental, yet the precise threshold for decline between typical inclusion levels (e.g., 18% and 36%) remains poorly defined. Determining this optimal level is crucial for formulating cost-effective, health-promoting diets that maximize growth without compromising physiological resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a non-linear relationship exists between dietary cornstarch level and tilapia performance, with a distinct inclusion threshold beyond which growth, tissue mineralization, antioxidant capacity, and immune function significantly decline.
proposals.method: A controlled feeding trial will be conducted using juvenile Nile tilapia. Six isonitrogenous and isoenergetic diets will be formulated with graded levels of cornstarch: 18%, 22%, 26%, 30%, 34%, and 36%. Following a randomized block design, fish will be fed their respective diets for 8 weeks. Growth performance (SGR, FCR), tissue mineralization (ash, zinc content via ICP-MS), antioxidant activity (SOD, CAT, MDA in liver), and immune response (lysozyme activity, respiratory burst activity in head kidney leukocytes) will be measured. Data will be analyzed using polynomial regression and broken-line regression models to identify breakpoints.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a precise, quantitative recommendation for the optimal dietary cornstarch level in tilapia feeds, directly informing commercial feed formulation. It will advance the understanding of carbohydrate metabolism in fish by linking specific inclusion levels to a suite of health biomarkers, bridging the gap between nutrition and physiological status.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has compared broad dietary categories, this study systematically tests intermediate cornstarch levels to pinpoint the exact threshold of decline using advanced broken-line analysis, integrating growth data with nuanced biochemical and immune parameters from Wang's recent methodological toolkit.

proposals.title: Dietary Cornstarch Modulates Gut Microbiota Composition, Metabolic Function, and Host Health in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus)
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, cornstarch is a common, low-cost carbohydrate source in aquafeeds, but its optimal inclusion level for gut health in Nile tilapia remains unclear. Given the established link between gut microbiota, nutrient metabolism, and immune function in fish, understanding how graded cornstarch levels reshape the microbial community is critical for formulating sustainable, health-promoting diets that do not compromise fish welfare or performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that increasing dietary cornstarch levels will induce significant, non-linear shifts in the tilapia gut microbiome, favoring bacterial taxa with enhanced carbohydrate metabolism, which will correlate with changes in host digestive efficiency, antioxidant status, and immune markers.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 10-week feeding trial with juvenile Nile tilapia fed isonitrogenous diets containing 0%, 10%, 20%, and 30% cornstarch. Gut microbiota will be profiled via 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing (V3-V4 region) on the Illumina MiSeq platform. Data will be analyzed using QIIME2 (2024.2) for alpha/beta diversity and taxonomic composition, and PICRUSt2 will predict metagenomic functional pathways. These microbial data will be integrated with pre-2026 growth performance, plasma antioxidant (SOD, CAT) activity, and immune (lysozyme, TNF-α) parameters from the same fish using Spearman correlation and redundancy analysis (RDA).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a detailed map linking specific cornstarch levels to defined microbial consortia and their predicted functions in tilapia intestine. It will establish evidence-based inclusion thresholds for cornstarch that support a beneficial microbiome, contributing directly to the fields of nutritional immunology and sustainable aquaculture feed formulation.
proposals.novelty: While past studies examined growth response to starch, this work uniquely integrates deep 16S sequencing, in-silico functional prediction, and host physiological biomarkers to mechanistically link dietary carbohydrate level to microbiome-mediated health outcomes in tilapia, moving beyond descriptive composition analysis.

proposals.title: Dietary Cornstarch Modulation of Innate Immune Gene Expression in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus): A qRT-PCR Analysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, optimizing non-fishmeal carbohydrate sources like cornstarch is critical for sustainable aquaculture, but their impact on fish health beyond growth metrics is poorly understood. Given the established link between nutrition and immunity, and the commercial importance of Nile tilapia, a molecular-level investigation into how dietary carbohydrate levels influence immune competence is urgently needed to formulate robust, health-promoting diets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that graded levels of dietary cornstarch will significantly alter the expression of key innate immune genes (e.g., IL-1β, TNF-α, lysozyme) in the head kidney and liver of Nile tilapia, with an optimal level enhancing immune readiness and excessive levels potentially causing immunosuppressive or inflammatory stress.
proposals.method: Nile tilapia will be fed isonitrogenous diets with graded cornstarch levels (e.g., 10%, 20%, 30%, 40%) for 8 weeks. Post-feeding, head kidney and liver tissues will be sampled for RNA extraction. Expression of target immune genes (IL-1β, TNF-α, Lysozyme, Hepcidin) will be quantified using quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR) with SYBR Green chemistry. The 2−ΔΔCt method will be used for relative quantification, normalizing data to stable reference genes (β-actin, EF1α). Statistical analysis will employ one-way ANOVA followed by Duncan's multiple range test.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed molecular profile linking specific cornstarch levels to innate immune status in Nile tilapia, moving beyond traditional growth and biochemical assays. The findings will directly inform feed formulations to optimize both economic and health outcomes, contributing to the 'functional feed' paradigm in aquaculture.
proposals.novelty: While Wang's prior work examined zinc nanoparticles and organic zinc supplements on immunity, this proposal uniquely focuses on the foundational macronutrient—carbohydrate—as an immune modulator. It applies established molecular techniques (qRT-PCR) to a novel nutritional variable (cornstarch level) within the researcher's existing fish model and dietary framework.

proposals.title: Metabolic Flux Analysis of Carbon Allocation from Dietary Cornstarch to Hepatic Lipid Synthesis in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) Using Stable Isotope Tracers
proposals.motivation: In aquaculture, cornstarch is a common, low-cost carbohydrate source, but its metabolic fate and contribution to lipid deposition in Nile tilapia are not fully quantified. Understanding this carbon flow is critical for optimizing feed formulations to enhance growth performance and energy efficiency while potentially mitigating excessive fat accumulation. Pre-2026, metabolic flux analysis (MFA) using stable isotopes is a powerful but underutilized tool in fish nutrition for mapping in vivo metabolic pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of dietary cornstarch-derived carbon is channeled through hepatic glycolysis and the citrate shuttle into de novo lipogenesis, and that this flux is modulated by dietary zinc status, given its known role in carbohydrate and lipid metabolism.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled feeding trial with juvenile Nile tilapia using diets containing 13C-glucose (a direct cornstarch digestion product) as a metabolic tracer. Fish will be sampled at strategic time points, and metabolites (glucose, lactate, citrate, acetyl-CoA, fatty acids) will be extracted from liver and muscle. Isotopic enrichment and labeling patterns will be analyzed via GC-MS. Metabolic network models for central carbon metabolism will be constructed, and flux distributions will be calculated using established computational platforms like INCA or 13C-FLUX, applying isotopomer balancing and least-squares regression to estimate reaction rates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative flux map of cornstarch carbon utilization in tilapia, identifying key regulatory nodes in lipogenesis. It will directly link dietary carbohydrate input to lipid output, offering a mechanistic basis for precision nutrition. The findings will also establish a foundational MFA methodology for future studies on functional feed additives, such as zinc nanoparticles or plant polyphenols, which may modulate these metabolic fluxes.
proposals.novelty: While Wang's prior work examined zinc supplements' effects on growth and immunity, applying dynamic 13C-MFA to trace real-time carbon fate from a specific dietary component in tilapia is novel. This integrates advanced biochemical tracing with aquaculture nutrition, moving beyond correlative measures to causal, quantitative metabolic engineering.

proposals.title: Validation of Zinc-Gluconate and Dihydromyricetin Nano-Supplements on Growth Performance, Immune Response, and Disease Resistance in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) Under Semi-Intensive Pond Culture
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has demonstrated the efficacy of novel dietary supplements, such as zinc-gluconate and nano-encapsulated dihydromyricetin (DMY), in enhancing growth, immunity, and antioxidant status in fish under controlled laboratory conditions. However, a critical gap exists in validating these promising results in commercial-scale, semi-intensive aquaculture systems where environmental stressors, variable water quality, and pathogen loads can significantly alter outcomes. Scaling these interventions is essential to translate academic findings into practical, economically viable strategies for sustainable aquaculture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dietary supplementation with zinc-gluconate and PEGylated DMY nanoliposomes will significantly improve the growth performance, innate immune parameters, and resistance to bacterial challenge (e.g., Aeromonas hydrophila) in Nile tilapia reared in commercial-scale ponds, replicating and extending the benefits observed in prior laboratory studies.
proposals.method: The study will employ a randomized complete block design in six earthen ponds (approx. 0.1 ha each). Juvenile Nile tilapia will be stocked at semi-intensive density and fed one of three isonitrogenous and isoenergetic diets for 12 weeks: a control (basal diet), T1 (basal + zinc-gluconate at optimized lab dose), and T2 (basal + zinc-gluconate + PEGylated DMY nanoliposomes coated with tea saponin). Key metrics include weekly growth sampling (weight, length, FCR), fortnightly hematological/immunological assays (lysozyme, phagocytosis, respiratory burst), and tissue mineralization analysis post-trial. A controlled challenge with A. hydrophila will be conducted on a subsample to assess disease resistance. Water quality parameters (DO, pH, temperature, ammonia) will be monitored biweekly as covariates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical data on the application and efficacy of combined zinc and nano-DMY supplements in a real-world aquaculture production setting. It will deliver practical dosage recommendations and management insights for semi-intensive tilapia farming, directly contributing to the development of functional feeds that reduce antibiotic use. The findings will bridge a critical gap between nutraceutical research and commercial aquaculture practice.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies by Wang et al. tested these supplements individually in tanks, this proposal uniquely investigates their synergistic effect at commercial scale and evaluates their efficacy against a natural disease challenge in a complex pond environment, moving beyond controlled microbial inhibition assays to integrated health validation.

proposals.title: Inverse Nutritional Programming: A Multi-Objective Optimization Framework to Design High-Starch, Energy-Dense Diets for Minimizing Lipid Deposition in Hybrid Grouper (Epinephelus fuscoguttatus ♀ × E. lanceolatus ♂)
proposals.motivation: In aquaculture, formulating cost-effective, energy-dense feeds often relies on high-starch ingredients like cornstarch, which paradoxically promotes hepatic lipid deposition and metabolic stress in carnivorous fish like grouper, compromising health and product quality. Pre-2026 nutritional studies primarily focus on linear dose-response relationships, lacking systematic methods to exploit this lipidogenic effect for beneficial outcomes. This creates a critical need for an inverse design approach to precisely structure diets that harness starch's energy density while actively counteracting its adverse fattening effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating the known lipid-increasing effect of dietary starch with targeted nutrient modulators (e.g., zinc nanoparticles, dihydromyricetin) into a computational inverse optimization model, we can derive novel diet formulations that maximize energy intake and growth while minimizing whole-body and hepatic lipid accumulation in hybrid grouper.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-objective genetic algorithm to solve the inverse diet formulation problem. Decision variables will include levels of cornstarch, zinc sources (gluconate vs. nano), and dihydromyricetin carriers (nanoparticle vs. nanoliposome). The algorithm will be trained on a meta-model built from our prior dose-response data on growth, tissue mineralization, antioxidant activity, and immune response. The optimization objectives are to maximize predicted weight gain and feed efficiency while minimizing predicted whole-body lipid percentage and hepatosomatic index, subject to nutritional constraints. The top Pareto-optimal formulations will be validated in a 10-week feeding trial with hybrid grouper.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational-experimental framework for 'inverse nutritional programming,' moving beyond descriptive nutrition to prescriptive diet design. It will produce specific, validated diet formulations that achieve high energy density with low lipid deposition, directly addressing a core dilemma in grouper feed formulation. The methodology will provide a new template for precision aquaculture nutrition applicable to other high-value species.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating the undesirable lipidogenic side-effect of starch as a controllable variable within an inverse optimization problem, explicitly designing diets to counteract it, rather than merely mitigating it. This combines pre-2026 concepts of nutrient-nutrient interactions, bioactive compounds (Zn, DHM), and computational optimization in a uniquely goal-oriented manner not previously applied to aquaculture diet formulation.

proposals.title: Environmental Fate and Aquatic Toxicity of Butenolide Degradation Products: A Dietary Exposure Study in Nile Tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus)
proposals.motivation: Butenolide-class antifouling agents are effective but raise environmental concerns due to their inherent toxicity. Pre-2026 research focuses on the parent compound, largely neglecting the environmental transformation products formed via hydrolysis and photolysis. It is critical to determine if these degradation pathways truly detoxify the compound or produce metabolites with residual bioactivity that could impact non-target aquatic organisms, particularly fish.
proposals.hypothesis: The primary toxicity of butenolide is inherent to its parent molecular structure, and its major environmental degradation products will exhibit significantly reduced or negligible acute toxicity and sub-lethal effects in a model fish species.
proposals.method: We will first simulate environmental degradation of butenolide via controlled hydrolysis and UV photolysis, characterizing major products using HPLC-MS. Following Wang's expertise, we will formulate experimental diets using cornstarch-based binders to incorporate precise doses of the parent compound and its isolated degradation products. Juvenile Nile tilapia (O. niloticus) will be exposed through dietary supplementation in a controlled study. We will assess growth performance, tissue bioaccumulation, hepatic antioxidant activity (SOD, CAT, MDA), and innate immune response (lysozyme, complement activity) to evaluate sub-lethal toxicity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first targeted dataset on the ecotoxicological profile of butenolide's environmental transformation products, moving beyond the parent compound. It will establish a dietary exposure model for antifouling biocide metabolites in fish, linking environmental chemistry to sub-organismal physiological responses. The findings will inform more accurate environmental risk assessments for this class of compounds.
proposals.novelty: While Wang's prior work studied dietary zinc and nanoparticle toxicity in tilapia, applying this dietary exposure model and biomarker suite to the fate of an organic antifouling agent's degradation products is novel. The approach integrates environmental chemistry simulation with in vivo fish toxicology to test the specific 'inherent toxicity' hypothesis, which is not addressed in the existing literature.

proposals.title: Assessing the Trophic Transfer and Sublethal Toxicity of Butenolide Antifouling Agents in Non-Target Marine Invertebrates: A Focus on Mollusks and Echinoderms
proposals.motivation: Butenolides are a promising class of antifouling compounds, but their environmental risk assessment is incomplete, focusing primarily on target fouling organisms and fish. Pre-2026 studies highlight significant gaps regarding their effects on ecologically and commercially vital non-target groups like mollusks and echinoderms, which are crucial for benthic-pelagic coupling and marine biodiversity. Understanding sublethal impacts on these species is essential for predicting ecosystem-level consequences of antifouling biocides.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that butenolides will induce significant sublethal toxicity in non-target mollusks and echinoderms, manifesting as oxidative stress, immune suppression, and disrupted energy metabolism, and that these effects will be modulated by dietary exposure pathways.
proposals.method: The study will employ a controlled dietary exposure model, incorporating butenolide into a cornstarch-bound feed matrix for the filter-feeder Crassostrea gigas (Pacific oyster) and the deposit-feeder Paracentrotus lividus (sea urchin). Building on Wang's recent methods, we will synthesize PEGylated nano-formulations of butenolide to ensure uniform dietary delivery. Sublethal endpoints will include tissue-specific bioaccumulation, antioxidant enzyme activity (SOD, CAT), immune parameters (lysozyme, phagocytic activity), and cellular energy allocation measured via metabolic profiling. A parallel acute toxicity assay will establish LC50 values for comparative risk.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset on the sublethal physiological impacts of butenolides on key marine invertebrates, directly informing environmental risk assessments for this biocide class. It will elucidate the role of trophic transfer via contaminated feed, a critical but understudied exposure route. The findings will contribute to developing more ecologically sustainable antifouling strategies by identifying vulnerable non-target species.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating Wang's expertise in dietary nanocarrier delivery (from zinc and polyphenol studies) to investigate the trophic toxicity of an emerging antifouling agent, a methodological synergy not previously applied in this context. It shifts the focus from acute lethality in fish to chronic, diet-mediated sublethal effects in phylogenetically distant, ecologically important benthic invertebrates.

ideas.title: Diet-Modulated Intestinal Targeting and Efficacy of Probiotic-Functionalized ZnO/EFS Nanohybrids
ideas.description: This study investigates how different basal dietary compositions (e.g., high-fat, high-fiber, or standard chow) modulate the in vivo biodistribution, intestinal targeting, and consequent therapeutic efficacy of drug-loaded ZnO/EFS nanohybrids that are functionally coupled with a specific probiotic strain. This approach reveals critical diet-nanoprobe-probiotic interactions, identifying optimal or contraindicated dietary contexts for maximizing the therapeutic potential of such advanced delivery systems.

ideas.title: Decoupling Probiotic Efficacy from Diet via a Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic Framework
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that probiotic efficacy is inherently dependent on or modulated by the host's basal diet. By drawing an analogy from clinical pharmacology, we adapt the solution of using a Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic (PK-PD) modeling framework. In drug development, this method isolates a drug's action from confounding factors like co-medications. Here, we model the probiotic's in vivo activity (e.g., persistence, metabolic output) as the PK component and the measured health benefit as the PD component. By conducting an intervention with systematically varied feed compositions and applying this analytical model, we can quantitatively determine if the diet alters the core PK-PD relationship of the probiotic. This allows a definitive test of whether the probiotic's mechanism of action is robust across dietary contexts or is fundamentally diet-dependent.

ideas.title: Engineering Probiotic Persistence via Environmental Pre-Selection
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that standard in vitro assays predict in vivo probiotic performance. Drawing an analogy from environmental engineering's method of pre-adapting microbes for bioremediation, the research adapts the solution of 'environmental pre-selection.' The adapted idea is to engineer probiotic persistence by creating a sophisticated in vitro simulation of the fish gastrointestinal tract environment, incorporating its key biochemical and biological stressors in a sequential, dynamic manner. Probiotic candidates are subjected to serial passage in this simulated environment, and only those demonstrating robust resistance and growth are then tested in vivo. This approach guarantees in vivo survival by using directed in vitro evolution to pre-adapt strains to the specific pressures they will encounter, turning the measurement of survival into a predictive, selective guarantee.

ideas.title: High-throughput cross-species screening of probiotic-derived antimicrobials for marine aquaculture
ideas.description: This research direction employs high-throughput automated platforms to screen secretions from candidate probiotic bacteria against pathogen libraries derived from multiple, economically important marine fish species simultaneously. This approach moves beyond testing whole probiotics in single hosts to rapidly deconvolve which specific antimicrobial compounds are effective against which pathogens in which host contexts. The resulting data matrix will reveal patterns of pathogen susceptibility and host-specific efficacy, informing the selection or engineering of probiotics with tailored activity spectra. Ultimately, this enables the development of precision probiotic formulations designed for specific polyculture environments or to address multi-species disease outbreaks.

ideas.description: This research fuses biosensor-based tracking with dose optimization by engineering luminescent B. subtilis 7k to serve as a real-time, in vivo reporter of colonization success. The study will correlate different probiotic doses and feeding periods with spatiotemporal colonization patterns, host immune gene expression, and growth metrics in live fish. This approach allows for the determination of a minimal effective dose not just based on final outcomes, but on the achievement of a defined, measurable colonization threshold required for benefit. Consequently, it establishes a precise, data-driven framework for probiotic administration that links dosage and duration directly to colonization dynamics and host response.

ideas.title: Adaptive Dose-Finding via Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the dose-finding methodology from clinical pharmacology to animal nutrition. The analogous problem of determining the minimal effective dose and optimal dosing schedule for a new drug is solved using Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) modeling. We propose to administer a feed additive at varying doses and durations, then frequently measure its concentration in blood or target tissues (pharmacokinetics) and concurrently assay biomarkers for growth performance and immune status (pharmacodynamics). By modeling the relationship between tissue exposure levels and the magnitude/duration of the biological effects, we can mathematically identify the minimal effective tissue concentration and the precise feeding period required to achieve it, thereby defining a rigorously optimized dosage and administration protocol.

ideas.title: Adapting Bioreactor Scale-Up Principles for Probiotic Trials in Aquaculture Systems
ideas.description: This idea adapts the scale-up methodology from industrial biotechnology to aquaculture probiotic testing. In bioprocessing, moving from lab flasks to production bioreactors requires controlling critical parameters like oxygen transfer and mixing. The analogous solution uses pilot-scale systems to test performance under scaled conditions by maintaining key dimensionless numbers. Adapted to aquaculture, the research would first identify the critical 'bioreactor' parameters of a pond or net-pen system (e.g., water flow/turnover analogous to mixing time, stocking density/biomass analogous to cell density, organic load affecting microbial competition). Pond or pen trials would then be designed to systematically vary these parameters in a controlled manner, measuring probiotic colonization, pathogen inhibition, and host health outcomes. The goal is to move beyond a simple 'does it work in a big pond?' trial to building a predictive model of probiotic performance based on measurable environmental variables, enabling reliable scaling to diverse commercial operations.

ideas.title: Core-Shell Probiotic Delivery Systems for Environmental Resilience in Open-Water Aquaculture
ideas.description: This research direction develops and tests a novel inverted core-shell particle, with probiotics encapsulated in a polymer core and protective ZnO nanoparticles embedded in the shell, specifically for deployment in pond or open-net pen trials. The design leverages the shell's ZnO for UV shielding and antimicrobial protection during water column transit, while the polymer core ensures probiotic viability and controlled release in the gut. This material innovation directly addresses the key challenge of environmental variable-induced probiotic inefficacy that plagues scale-up efforts, enabling a rigorous evaluation of whether advanced delivery systems can translate lab-probiotic benefits to real-world, commercial aquaculture productivity and disease management.

ideas.title: Single-Cell Probiotic Microcapsule Delivery for Larval Grouper Gut Microbiome Engineering
ideas.description: This research direction combines droplet microfluidics with larval grouper aquaculture to create and test monodisperse, single-probiotic-cell microcapsules designed for ingestion by early-life-stage fish. The platform allows for precise delivery of defined probiotic strains or consortia to the larval gut, enabling real-time tracking of individual encapsulated cell viability, colonization success, and spatial distribution within the immature gastrointestinal tract. This approach moves beyond bulk efficacy testing to uncover the fundamental colonization dynamics and host-microbe interactions that determine probiotic success in vulnerable juveniles, paving the way for rationally designed, stage-specific microbial therapies.

ideas.title: Cross-Species Probiotic Efficacy via Host-Microbiome Phylogenetic Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from pharmaceutical drug repurposing, where comparative genomics is used to predict drug efficacy across species based on target conservation. The analogous solution involves using phylogenetic analysis of drug targets to guide repurposing efforts. Adapted to this research context, the project will first construct a phylogenetic map of host factors (e.g., gut mucosal receptors, immune pathways) and core microbiome profiles across economically important marine fish. By analyzing the conservation of the specific host-microbe interaction mechanisms targeted by the probiotic, we can predict its likely efficacy in new species before empirical testing. This rational, phylogenetically-informed approach prioritizes testing in species with high mechanistic conservation, making the scaling process more efficient and scientifically grounded.

ideas.title: Dual-sensor, activity-gated B. subtilis biosensor for discriminating viable gut colonization in live fish
ideas.description: This redesigned approach engineers a B. subtilis 7k strain with a dual-reporter system to overcome the ambiguity of simple luminescence. A constitutively expressed luminescent gene provides real-time, whole-animal tracking of total bacterial presence. In parallel, a second, tightly regulated genetic circuit drives a fluorescent protein only when activated by specific environmental cues found in the host gut (e.g., bile salts), serving as a biomarker for successful colonization and metabolic activity. This allows researchers to non-invasively monitor general probiotic presence via bioluminescence imaging, then validate and quantify the viable, colonizing fraction by analyzing dissected gut samples via fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS), creating a more robust and interpretable colonization assay.

ideas.title: Phased safety screening using gnotobiotic larval models prior to probiotic challenge trials
ideas.description: This redesigned approach first establishes probiotic safety for early life stages through a tiered screening system. Initial in vitro assays using grouper larval cell lines assess cellular stress and immune responses to candidate probiotics. Promising candidates then advance to gnotobiotic larval models, where larvae with defined microbiomes are exposed under controlled conditions to evaluate impacts on development, survival, and microbiome stability. Only probiotics demonstrating safety and beneficial metabolic or immunological effects in these controlled models proceed to traditional challenge trials with conventional larvae, thereby minimizing risk to valuable larval stocks while identifying truly beneficial interventions.

ideas.title: Hydrophobically-Encapsulated Probiotics for Resilient Commercial Aquaculture
ideas.description: This research direction develops and tests probiotic encapsulation systems that rely on hydrophobic interactions or covalent bonds, rather than hydrogen bonds, to create a more stable protective matrix. This engineered stability is specifically targeted to maintain probiotic viability and function under the variable salinity, pH, temperature, and organic load conditions encountered in pond or open-net pen systems. By moving to commercial-scale trials with this advanced encapsulation, the work directly evaluates whether physicochemical resilience translates to consistent colonization and health benefits in real-world aquaculture environments. The synergy lies in using encapsulation chemistry to solve a core scalability problem, bridging the gap between laboratory efficacy and field-deployable commercial products.

ideas.title: Assess probiotic effects on gut microbiota using ecological network stability analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Community Ecology to the original probiotic research. In ecology, introducing a new species is studied by analyzing its impact on interaction networks and ecosystem stability. The adapted research idea is to analyze how B. subtilis 7k supplementation alters the intestinal microbial community by constructing and comparing microbial co-occurrence networks (pre- and post-supplementation) from sequencing data. This approach will identify changes in network properties (e.g., connectivity, modularity), pinpoint keystone taxa whose roles shift, and predict the overall impact on the community's stability and functional resilience, moving beyond simple compositional analysis.

ideas.title: Test probiotic synergy using a minimal community assembly model from ecology
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a multi-strain probiotic consortium is inherently superior to a single strain by adapting a solution from community ecology. The analogous field is Community Ecology, where the analogous solution is the 'minimal community assembly' approach used to dissect the contributions of individual species to ecosystem function. The adapted idea is to test probiotic synergy not with a static consortium comparison, but by building the microbial community stepwise. Starting with the single strain B. subtilis 7k, we systematically introduce additional strains with distinct, complementary metabolic or protective functions. By measuring host health parameters after each addition, we can rigorously determine if the proposed consortium provides synergistic benefits, or if the foundational single strain is sufficient and additional strains are redundant or even antagonistic.

ideas.title: A single-strain, multi-compartment probiotic system to test compartment-specific vs. synergistic benefits
ideas.description: This research direction investigates whether the perceived need for multi-strain probiotic consortia can be replaced by a single, versatile strain engineered for multi-compartment function. We will design a delivery system that first protects and activates B. subtilis 7k in the gastric environment to exert stomach-specific benefits (e.g., pathogen inhibition, pH modulation), then allows its spores to germinate and colonize the intestine for gut-specific effects. The core hypothesis is that by enabling a single strain to perform distinct, compartmentalized roles, we can achieve synergistic health benefits comparable to a multi-strain product, simplifying production and control while fundamentally testing the principle of probiotic synergy.

ideas.title: High-throughput screening of bacterial metabolites for controlled-release, gut-targeted antimicrobial formulations
ideas.description: This research direction integrates high-throughput screening of bacterial culture supernatants or purified metabolites against intestinal pathogen libraries with advanced material science for gut-targeted delivery. The most promising antimicrobial hits from the screening are then formulated into controlled-release systems, such as those based on EFS-ZnO matrices, where the crosslinking density and composition are fine-tuned to match the degradation timeline and pH of specific intestinal regions. This synergy allows for the discovery of novel antimicrobials from the human microbiome and their immediate optimization into formulations that maintain effective local concentrations over a prolonged period, maximizing efficacy while minimizing systemic exposure and resistance development. The outcome is a platform for developing next-generation, precision probiotics or synbiotics designed to treat or prevent enteric infections.

ideas.title: Adapting Phage Cocktail Synergy to Design a Multi-Pathogen Probiotic Defense for Grouper
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from bacteriophage therapy, where cocktails of phages are combined to create broad-spectrum, resilient treatments against complex infections, this idea adapts the 'cocktail' strategy to aquaculture probiotics. The analogous solution involves using synergistic combinations to overcome limitations of single-agent approaches. The adapted research idea is to design and evaluate a defined, multi-strain probiotic consortium, built around B. subtilis 7k, for protecting grouper. The consortium would combine complementary modes of action (e.g., bacteriocin production, competitive exclusion, immunomodulation, direct anti-parasitic activity) from different microbial species. The efficacy of this consortium would be tested against co-infections or sequential challenges with key bacterial and parasitic pathogens to determine if it provides superior, broad-spectrum protection compared to a monopropiotic application.

ideas.title: Systematic Deconstruction and Benchmarking of ZnO's Multifunctional Role in EFS Capsule Therapeutics
ideas.description: This adversarially improved research idea retains ZnO as the essential performance benchmark while rigorously challenging its status as an irreplaceable active. The core approach is to use the EFS capsule as a delivery vehicle for novel actives, but within a controlled experimental system that quantitatively dissects ZnO's individual therapeutic mechanisms. The study will design parallel formulation series with and without ZnO, pairing it with candidate replacements, to isolate and measure contributions to antimicrobial activity, anti-inflammatory action, and tissue barrier integrity. The final output is a validated, multifunctional performance map that defines the minimum excipient and active properties required to match the ZnO benchmark, thereby either justifying its replacement or conclusively demonstrating its indispensability.

ideas.title: Systematically deconvolute a complex antimicrobial secretome using a multi-omics and bioassay-guided fractionation pipeline
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes to solve the problem of identifying seven specific antimicrobial chemicals from a secretome by adapting the established pipeline from natural product discovery in complex environmental microbiomes. The analogous field tackles the challenge of finding bioactive needles in a haystack of microbial metabolites through an integrated workflow of bioassay-guided fractionation coupled with advanced analytical chemistry and omics. The adapted research idea applies this solution by designing an experimental pipeline where the conditioned medium from the antimicrobial-producing system is fractionated using HPLC, with each fraction tested for antimicrobial activity against a panel of target microbes. Active fractions are then characterized using LC-MS/MS for preliminary structural identification and purified for definitive NMR analysis, ultimately yielding the chemical structure and specific activity profile for each of the seven compounds. This systematic approach moves beyond mere detection to full characterization.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Design probiotics to resist it
ideas.description: Engineer beneficial bacterial strains with membranes resistant to TS/CTS@DMY-lips for use in competitive exclusion.

ideas.title: The Hormesis of Defense: Mapping the Dose-Response Curve of Chronic Immune Stimulation
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that immune enhancement is always beneficial long-term by drawing an analogy from toxicology. The analogous problem is the linear assumption that a toxin's harm increases directly with dose, which was solved by the concept of hormesis—a biphasic dose-response where low doses are beneficial and high doses harmful. Adapting this solution, the research will investigate the potential costs of chronic immune stimulation (like autoimmunity or metabolic trade-offs) by hypothesizing a hormetic relationship for immune activation. The study will systematically map the dose-response curve of specific immune stimuli (e.g., adjuvants, chronic infections, environmental triggers) against long-term health metrics, aiming to identify the inflection points where the net benefit of immune enhancement turns into a net cost due to excessive or chronic stimulation. This reframes the question from 'is enhancement good or bad?' to 'at what level and duration does enhancement become detrimental?'

ideas.title: Employ metabolomics to map metabolic disruption
ideas.description: Use untargeted metabolomics from systems biology to fully map the bacterial metabolic pathways inhibited by the nanoliposomes.

ideas.title: In vivo pathogen challenge as a direct assay for probiotic colonization and function
ideas.description: This research direction directly challenges grouper colonized with a fluorescently tagged B. subtilis 7k strain with specific bacterial or parasitic pathogens. By correlating the in vivo spatial distribution and density of the probiotic, measured via fluorescence and qPCR from dissected GI tract sections, with the degree of host protection against each pathogen, the study establishes causal, quantitative links between colonization patterns and functional efficacy. This moves beyond simply confirming survival or measuring gross mortality, instead identifying the 'colonization thresholds' and 'functional niches' required for protection against distinct disease agents. The approach transforms a pathogen challenge from an efficacy endpoint into a dynamic probe for understanding the in vivo mechanism of probiotic action.

ideas.title: Engineered Probiotic-Derived Vesicles for Targeted Anti-Virulence Therapy
ideas.description: This research direction first applies the 'inverse problem' screening to probiotic strains to identify unique, non-toxic bacterial surface structures (e.g., specific outer membrane proteins, pili, or vesicle compositions) that have evolved to interact with or evade specific pathogens. These identified structures are then harvested or mimicked to engineer the 'EFS capsule' delivery vehicle, stripping it of ZnO. The vehicle is loaded with narrow-spectrum anti-virulence agents, such as quorum-sensing inhibitors or specific protease blockers, which are only effective against the pathogen(s) targeted by the probiotic-derived surface structure. This results in a highly targeted therapeutic that uses probiotic ecology to guide delivery, selectively disarming pathogens while minimizing disruption to the commensal microbiome.

ideas.title: Factorial Consortium Analysis to Disentangle Synergy from Additivity for B. subtilis 7k
ideas.description: This study uses a full factorial experimental design to test all possible combinations of B. subtilis 7k and a defined set of candidate companion strains. Each strain and combination is tested in vitro and in a relevant animal model for multiple, mechanistically distinct outcomes. Statistical interaction models are applied to the resulting data to quantitatively identify significant synergistic or antagonistic interactions, separating them from simple additive effects. This approach definitively reveals whether a consortium provides a unique benefit beyond what the individual components contribute independently.

ideas.title: Characterize the Functional Ecology and Resistance Landscapes of Seven Secreted Antimicrobial Chemicals
ideas.description: This project moves beyond static chemical characterization to define the dynamic functional profiles of seven secreted antimicrobial compounds. It will quantify their activity under ecologically relevant conditions, including within biofilms and in the presence of host factors, and systematically assay for the in vitro emergence of microbial resistance. Finally, it will map chemical-genetic interaction networks to identify synergistic partners and predict compound combinations that could mitigate resistance development.

ideas.title: Deconvolve host-mediated from direct microbial mechanisms of B. subtilis 7k action on gut community structure
ideas.description: This study employs a multi-armed gnotobiotic mouse model to mechanistically dissect how B. subtilis 7k alters gut microbiota composition. One arm uses germ-free mice colonized with a defined human microbial consortium, with and without the probiotic, to assess direct microbe-microbe interactions. Parallel arms introduce the probiotic into the same consortium within hosts lacking key immune signaling pathways (e.g., MyD88-/-) to identify host-immune-dependent effects. Integrated metagenomics, metabolomics, and host transcriptomics from intestinal tissues are used to construct a causal model of probiotic action, separating direct ecological impact from indirect host-mediated modulation.

ideas.title: Engineered Antimicrobial Bioremediation: Structurally Optimized Secretomes for Pathogen-Suppressed Water Treatment
ideas.description: This research fuses the structural characterization of seven antimicrobial compounds from a microbial secretome with the bioremediation capability of B. subtilis 7k. The core objective is to structurally optimize these antimicrobials, either through biosynthesis tuning or semi-synthesis, to selectively inhibit key waterborne pathogens that impede bioremediation processes. The optimized antimicrobial suite would then be deployed in concert with B. subtilis 7k, creating a dual-action treatment system that actively manages the microbial community to suppress competitors and pathogens while simultaneously degrading organic pollutants. This approach moves beyond passive degradation to actively engineer the microbial ecology of contaminated water for enhanced and more reliable cleanup.

ideas.title: Adapt a multi-scale environmental impact assessment framework for biocompatibility testing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the tiered, multi-scale framework from environmental toxicology and ecological risk assessment to create a more robust and predictive biocompatibility evaluation protocol. The analogous field solves the problem of assessing complex environmental impact by using a phased approach: from simple in-vitro assays to complex in-vivo and ecosystem-level studies. The adapted research idea applies this by proposing a structured testing cascade for a new biomedical system. It begins with high-throughput in-vitro cytotoxicity and hemocompatibility screening against a diverse cell panel, progresses to intermediate complexity models like 3D co-cultures or organ-chips to study cell-system interactions, and finally uses targeted in-vivo studies to assess chronic immune response and integration. This creates a comprehensive, phased safety dossier that better predicts clinical performance.

ideas.title: Contained, Immobilized B. subtilis 7k Bioremediation Cassettes for Safe Water Treatment
ideas.description: This research investigates the use of Bacillus subtilis 7k, immobilized within a retrievable and biodegradable hydrogel matrix, for the targeted degradation of waste compounds in water. The encapsulated bacteria are housed in permeable cassettes, allowing for controlled bioremediation while preventing the environmental release of live cells. The system is designed to monitor degradation efficiency and structural integrity in real-time, enabling safe deployment and retrieval. This approach directly mitigates the ecological risks of introducing free-living bacteria, creating a more robust and environmentally secure bioremediation technology.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Transcriptomic Screening to Identify Immuno-Antimicrobial Probiotic Effectors
ideas.description: This approach integrates high-throughput screening of probiotic bacterial secretions against pathogen libraries with parallel host transcriptomic profiling. Automated platforms are used to screen thousands of probiotic strain supernatants or purified fractions, but instead of selecting hits based solely on antimicrobial activity, each candidate is simultaneously applied to host immune cells for RNA-seq analysis. This dual-output screening identifies secretions that not only kill or inhibit pathogens but also induce a specific, beneficial immune pathway signature in the host. The synergy enables the discovery of novel probiotic effector molecules that function through a combined mechanism of direct antimicrobial action and targeted immune modulation, offering a more robust therapeutic strategy against complex infections.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Probiotic-Induced Immune Pathway Activation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Ecology—Species Distribution Modeling (SDM)—to the problem of mapping immune pathways. In ecology, SDMs correlate species presence with environmental variables to predict a suitable niche. Here, we apply this conceptual framework to transcriptomic data. We will use RNA-seq data from infected tissues with and without probiotic pretreatment. Instead of a simple differential expression list, we will treat the collective expression of immune pathway genes (e.g., from inflammasome, interferon, or lymphocyte activation pathways) as multivariate 'environmental layers'. Statistical or machine learning models will then be used to correlate the 'presence' of a protective outcome with a specific multi-gene expression signature, thereby defining and mapping the precise 'transcriptional immune niche' activated by the probiotic that confers resistance during infection.

ideas.title: Engineered B. subtilis 7k as a 'Living Filter' with Tunable Degradation Pathways
ideas.description: This idea expands the investigation of B. subtilis 7k for water bioremediation by drawing an analogy from industrial catalysis. In that field, complex problems involving mixed feedstocks are solved using engineered catalyst supports with multiple, tunable active sites. Adapting this solution, the research would engineer B. subtilis 7k as a 'living filter' with programmable degradation pathways. Genetic modules for degrading distinct waste compounds (e.g., hydrocarbons, nitrates) would be integrated alongside biosensor circuits. This allows the bacterium to dynamically respond to the specific contaminant profile of a water source, activating only the necessary enzymatic pathways. This approach aims to create a more efficient, versatile, and self-regulating bioremediation agent capable of handling the complex and variable mixtures found in real-world wastewater, moving beyond single-compound degradation.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporally Resolved In Vivo Study of Site-Specific Drug Release and Efficacy from ZnO/EFS Nanohybrids
ideas.description: This study employs a dual-radiolabeled nanohybrid system to independently track the ZnO/EFS carrier and the therapeutic payload in real-time using longitudinal SPECT/CT imaging in live animal models. Concurrently, intestinal cannulation allows for direct sampling of luminal and mucosal drug concentrations at target sites to quantify local release kinetics. This approach definitively links the precise anatomical location of carrier accumulation with the site and magnitude of drug release, separating targeting efficiency from systemic biodistribution. Efficacy metrics (e.g., local cytokine levels, histological scoring) are then directly correlated with these spatially resolved pharmacokinetic data, providing an unambiguous assessment of intestinal targeting success and therapeutic mechanism.

ideas.title: Closed-loop, phenotype-guided adaptive dosing for robust growth and immunity
ideas.description: This research develops a precision administration system that dynamically personalizes dosage and treatment duration based on real-time host response. It employs continuous biosensor and periodic multi-omic profiling to quantify growth efficiency and immune status, building predictive models of individual trajectories. A control algorithm uses this feedback to adjust administration parameters in a closed loop, aiming to maintain optimal states despite individual variation and environmental perturbations. The outcome is an adaptive protocol that maximizes efficacy and robustness across a heterogeneous population, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all optimum.
